title: Mode-Shape Expansion Using Physics-Constrained Gaussian Process Regression
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of reconstructing full-field structural mode shapes from sparse sensor data. While Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) offers a robust non-parametric framework for spatial interpolation and uncertainty quantification, standard formulations often yield physically inconsistent mode-shape reconstructions under sparse sensing conditions. A Physics-Constrained Single-Output Gaussian Process (CONS-SOGP) framework is derived that utilizes independent modal kernels while coupling the optimization via a mass-orthogonality penalty. The paper presents derivations for the marginal likelihood, hyperparameter gradients, and penalty coupling. Numerical verification on a multi-degree-of-freedom structure demonstrates that the proposed method overcomes existing limitations in GP-based prediction, providing more accurate and reliable expanded mode shapes.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a Physics-Constrained Single-Output Gaussian Process (CONS-SOGP) that integrates mass-orthogonality constraints into GPR to reconstruct physically consistent full-field mode shapes from sparse sensor data.
AI.motivation: Standard Gaussian Process Regression often produces physically inconsistent mode-shape reconstructions when working with sparse sensor data, limiting its practical utility in structural dynamics.
AI.method: The proposed CONS-SOGP framework uses independent modal kernels and couples optimization through a mass-orthogonality penalty, with derivations for marginal likelihood, hyperparameter gradients, and penalty coupling.
AI.result: Numerical verification on a multi-degree-of-freedom structure shows the method overcomes existing GP-based prediction limitations, yielding more accurate and reliable expanded mode shapes.
AI.conclusion: The physics-constrained approach significantly improves the physical consistency and accuracy of mode-shape reconstructions from sparse measurements compared to standard GPR.

title: High-order Conservative Discontinuous Galerkin Methods via Implicit Penalization for the Generalized Korteweg-de Vries Equation and the Hirota-Satsuma KdV System
summary: We develop new conservative discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods for nonlinear wave problems, focusing on the generalized Korteweg-de Vries (gKdV) equation and the coupled Hirota-Satsuma KdV (HS-KdV) system. The proposed methods preserve mass through the single-valued structure of numerical traces, while energy and Hamiltonian conservation are enforced by implicitly determining penalty parameters in the numerical traces through auxiliary conservation constraints. In our previous work [11], we developed a conservative DG method for the gKdV equation; however, that formulation involves the time derivative of the jump of the approximate solution, which complicates extensions beyond second-order temporal accuracy. Our new formulation overcomes this limitation by introducing a redesigned trace configuration that eliminates the derivative-of-jump term. This novel enhancement seamlessly paves the way for higher-order time discretizations and requires solving fewer nonlinear systems per time step than the previous approach. For the coupled HS-KdV system, we present the first conservative DG method that preserves all three invariants of the exact solution. Numerical results demonstrate the accuracy and expected convergence behavior of the proposed methods, as well as long-time stability and strong conservation properties for both the gKdV equation and HS-KdV system.

AI.tldr: This paper develops new conservative discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods for nonlinear wave problems (gKdV equation and HS-KdV system) that preserve mass, energy, and Hamiltonian by using redesigned numerical traces without derivative-of-jump terms, enabling higher-order time discretizations and improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of prior conservative DG methods for gKdV equations that involve time derivatives of solution jumps, which complicate extensions beyond second-order temporal accuracy, and to develop the first conservative DG method for the coupled HS-KdV system preserving all three invariants.
AI.method: New conservative DG methods with redesigned trace configurations that eliminate derivative-of-jump terms; mass preserved via single-valued numerical traces; energy and Hamiltonian conservation enforced by implicitly determining penalty parameters through auxiliary conservation constraints.
AI.result: Numerical results confirm accuracy, expected convergence behavior, long-time stability, and strong conservation properties for both the gKdV equation and HS-KdV system.
AI.conclusion: The new formulation overcomes the derivative-of-jump limitation, enables higher-order time discretizations, requires solving fewer nonlinear systems per time step than prior approaches, and presents the first conservative DG method preserving all three invariants for the HS-KdV system.

title: Fourier--Galerkin Methods for Subwavelength Resonances in 2D Acoustic Metamaterials
summary:
We present a Fourier--Galerkin framework for the analysis and computation of subwavelength resonances in two-dimensional scattering problems in finite domains. Starting from the boundary integral formulation, we project the operator onto Fourier modes and derive an explicit finite-dimensional effective matrix whose singularity characterizes the resonant frequencies. In the subwavelength regime, we obtain asymptotic expansions of this matrix in terms of $ω$ and the material contrast, identifying the leading-order operators and their kernel structure.
  This reduction transforms the resonance problem into a low-dimensional nonlinear eigenvalue problem, avoiding large-scale discretizations and global root-search procedures. The entries of the effective matrix are explicitly computable and admit fast evaluation using FFT-based quadrature. The resulting approach provides an efficient and robust computational framework for resonances in general smooth geometries.

AI.tldr: A Fourier-Galerkin framework reduces boundary integral formulations of subwavelength resonances to a low-dimensional nonlinear eigenvalue problem, enabling efficient computation via FFT-based quadrature without large-scale discretizations.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and robust computational method for subwavelength resonances in 2D scattering problems that avoids expensive large-scale discretizations and global root-searching procedures.
AI.method: Projects boundary integral operators onto Fourier modes to derive an explicit finite-dimensional effective matrix; uses asymptotic expansions in ω and material contrast; transforms the resonance problem into a low-dimensional nonlinear eigenvalue problem with FFT-based quadrature for fast evaluation.
AI.result: The effective matrix's singularity characterizes resonant frequencies; the method works for general smooth geometries and avoids large-scale discretizations and global root-search.
AI.conclusion: The Fourier-Galerkin framework provides an efficient and robust computational approach for subwavelength resonances in 2D scattering problems with smooth geometries.

title: A matrix-based spectral method for the numerical approximation of the fractional Laplacian and the fractional $p$-Laplacian of functions defined on $\mathbb R^n$
summary:
Given a function $u$ defined on $\mathbb R^n$, its fractional $p$-Laplacian is given by $$(-Δ)_p^su(\vec x)=C_1(n,s,p)\int_{\mathbb R^n}\frac{|u(\vec x)-u(\vec y)|^{p-2}(u(\vec x)-u(\vec y))}{\|\vec x-\vec y\|_2^{n+sp}}d\vec y,\quad\vec x\in\mathbb R^n,$$where the integral is understood in the principal value sense, $p\in(1,\infty)$, $s\in(0,1)$, and $C_1(n,s,p)$ is a normalization constant. A formally equivalent nonlinear Balakrishnan formulation is given by $$(-Δ)_p^su(\vec x)=C_4(n,s,p)\int_0^\inftyΔ(t-Δ)^{-1}\left[Φ_p(u(\vec x)-u(\cdot))\right](\vec x)\frac{dt}{t^{1-sp/2}},$$ where $C_4(n,s,p)$ is another normalization constant, and $Φ_p(t)=|t|^{p-2}t$.
  In this paper, we present a matrix-based spectral method to approximate numerically the fractional Laplacian (i.e., the linear case, where $p = 2$) and the fractional $p$-Laplacian for functions defined on $\mathbb R^n$. Our approach builds on the Balakrishnan representation, where we discretize the 2nd-order derivatives in $Δ$ using spectrally accurate differentiation matrices. A key advantage is that these matrices can be diagonalized in a well-conditioned manner, enabling a stable and robust numerical scheme that naturally extends to arbitrary spatial dimensions $n$. In particular, this diagonalization allows the fractional operator to act directly on the eigenvalue spectrum, effectively reducing the Balakrishnan integral to an analytical evaluation at the spectral level and thereby avoiding costly multidimensional quadrature. The resulting method also avoids domain truncation and variational formulations, making it both computationally efficient and conceptually straightforward.
  As a practical application, we simulate the evolution of $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}+(-Δ)^s_pu=0,$$in one and two spatial dimensions, being able to capture the self-similar solutions that arise as $t\to\infty$.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a matrix-based spectral method for numerically approximating the fractional Laplacian and fractional p-Laplacian using the Balakrishnan representation. The method diagonalizes differentiation matrices to evaluate the Balakrishnan integral analytically at the spectral level, avoiding costly quadrature. It works in arbitrary dimensions and is demonstrated on evolution equations capturing self-similar solutions.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient, stable, and dimension-independent numerical method for computing the fractional p-Laplacian that avoids costly multidimensional quadrature, domain truncation, and variational formulations.
AI.method: The method discretizes second-order derivatives in the Balakrishnan representation using spectrally accurate differentiation matrices, which are diagonalized in a well-conditioned manner. This allows the fractional operator to act on the eigenvalue spectrum, reducing the Balakrishnan integral to an analytical evaluation at the spectral level.
AI.result: The method avoids domain truncation and variational formulations, extends naturally to arbitrary spatial dimensions n, and is demonstrated on 1D and 2D evolution equations of the form ∂u/∂t + (-Δ)_p^s u = 0, capturing self-similar solutions as t→∞.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral method is computationally efficient, stable, robust, and conceptually straightforward for numerically approximating the fractional p-Laplacian in arbitrary dimensions.

title: On the final-state problem for the 1D cubic NLS
summary:
We consider the one-dimensional cubic nonlinear Schrödinger equation $$ \ii\partial_tu+\frac12\partial_{xx}u=\la|u|^2u,\,λ=\pm 1 $$ and solve the final-state (modified wave operator) problem for small asymptotic data. More precisely, given a small $W(ξ)$, we construct a solution $u$ such that \begin{equation*}
  u\rightarrow (2π)^{-1/2}(\ii t)^{-1/2}e^{\ii x^2/(2t)}\, W\!\Big(\frac{x}{t}\Big)\exp(-\ii\la|W(x/t)|^2\log t). \end{equation*} Crucially, we design a contraction map, so that we can run the analysis in the spirit of Kato--Pusateri \cite{KP} for $w$ with a forcing term depending {\it only} on the final data $W$. This scheme is easy to adapt to solving final state problems with a complete theory for the forward problems.

AI.tldr: This paper solves the final-state (modified wave operator) problem for the 1D cubic nonlinear Schrödinger equation with small asymptotic data, using a contraction map approach inspired by Kato-Pusateri that depends only on the final data W.
AI.motivation: To solve the final-state problem for the 1D cubic NLS equation, i.e., constructing solutions that match prescribed asymptotic scattering data with logarithmic phase correction.
AI.method: Designs a contraction map where the forcing term depends only on the final data W, following the Kato-Pusateri framework, enabling backward analysis from asymptotic data.
AI.result: Successfully constructs a solution u converging to the modified scattering state with logarithmic phase factor exp(-iλ|W|² log t) for small given W(ξ).
AI.conclusion: The contraction scheme is easy to adapt for solving final state problems with a complete theory for forward problems.

title: Full-Scattering-Matrix Deterministic Phonon Boltzmann Transport Simulation
summary: Solutions to the phonon Boltzmann transport equation under the relaxation-time approximation (RTA) are fundamentally limited in that they do not account for the off-diagonal elements of the scattering matrix, which encode intermode energy redistribution. We find that the phonon in-scattering matrix is globally incompressible, requiring nearly its full rank for any useful Frobenius accuracy. The incompressibility worsens as the Brillouin zone is refined. We show that, despite this difficulty, one can develop a computationally efficient 3D BTE solver incorporating the complete scattering matrix by leveraging our two structural discoveries: the non-equilibrium phonon distribution inhabits a remarkably low-dimensional subspace of mode space regardless of how many phonon modes exist, and the leading singular modes of the scattering operator align selectively with this transport-active subspace. Consequently, truncation incurs negligible transport error even under large norm error. The phonon streaming operator's mode-diagonal character further motivates a hybrid architecture that exploits these two properties. When applied to nanoscale structures emulating a fin field-effect transistor, our BTE solver quantifies a geometry-independent multiplicative correction to the temperature rise predicted under RTA. Our theoretical work and resulting BTE solver help enable rigorous study of phonon transport and systematic design of devices and structures in the ballistic and quasi-ballistic phonon transport regime.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a computationally efficient 3D phonon BTE solver that incorporates the full scattering matrix (beyond RTA) by exploiting two key discoveries: the non-equilibrium phonon distribution lives in a low-dimensional subspace, and the scattering operator's leading singular modes align with this subspace. Applied to nanoscale finFET-like structures, it reveals a geometry-independent multiplicative correction to RTA-predicted temperature rise.
AI.motivation: Standard RTA-based solutions to the phonon Boltzmann transport equation are fundamentally limited because they ignore off-diagonal elements of the scattering matrix that encode intermode energy redistribution, leading to inaccuracies especially in ballistic/quasi-ballistic regimes.
AI.method: The authors discover two structural properties: (1) the non-equilibrium phonon distribution inhabits a remarkably low-dimensional subspace of mode space, and (2) the leading singular modes of the scattering operator align selectively with this transport-active subspace. They leverage these to build a hybrid architecture BTE solver incorporating the complete scattering matrix.
AI.result: Despite the phonon in-scattering matrix being globally incompressible, the solver achieves negligible transport error under truncation. Applied to nanoscale finFET structures, it quantifies a geometry-independent multiplicative correction to RTA-predicted temperature rise.
AI.conclusion: The work enables rigorous study of phonon transport and systematic design of devices in ballistic and quasi-ballistic regimes, overcoming the fundamental limitations of RTA-based approaches.

title: Gyrokinetic Simulations for Spherical Tokamak Divertor Design
comment: The University of Texas at Austin PhD thesis
summary:
Nuclear fusion is an attractive source of energy because the fuel is abundant and it produces low levels of carbon emissions. The tokamak, which confines a plasma using magnetic fields, is the most mature nuclear fusion reactor concept. Maximizing energy confinement by minimizing turbulent heat loss while also minimizing damage to the reactor is essential for producing efficient, commercially viable fusion reactors.
  Heat exhaust methods used in the scrape-off layer (SOL) of the tokamak greatly influence performance. Conventional heat exhaust methods focus on minimizing reactor damage rather than maximizing confinement. The low-recycling regime, a newer approach, focuses on maximizing energy confinement. Studying the low-recycling regime, which features a high temperature and low density SOL, requires new modeling tools. We have developed the gyrokinetic code Gkeyll into an appropriate tool, and we use it to demonstrate the viability of the low-recycling regime with simulations of the Spherical Tokamak for Energy Production (STEP).
  Our work addresses several key issues with low recycling. Our simulation results indicate that a high SOL temperature and low SOL density could be achieved without using a lithium divertor plate. This is an important step because lithium divertor plates evaporate when exposed to large heat fluxes, which lowers the SOL temperature, counteracting the desired regime. Our simulation results also indicate that kinetic effects can lower the peak heat flux on the divertor plate, which would improve reactor survivability, and confine sputtered impurities to the divertor region, which would prevent core contamination and performance degradation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Gkeyll, a gyrokinetic simulation tool, to demonstrate the viability of the low-recycling regime in tokamaks like STEP, showing high SOL temperature and low density can be achieved without lithium divertor plates, with kinetic effects reducing heat flux and confining impurities.
AI.motivation: Conventional tokamak heat exhaust methods prioritize minimizing reactor damage over maximizing energy confinement, and studying the promising low-recycling regime requires new modeling tools that were previously unavailable.
AI.method: The authors developed the gyrokinetic code Gkeyll to model the low-recycling regime in tokamaks, and performed simulations of the Spherical Tokamak for Energy Production (STEP).
AI.result: Simulations showed: (1) high SOL temperature and low SOL density can be achieved without lithium divertor plates; (2) kinetic effects reduce peak heat flux on the divertor plate; (3) kinetic effects confine sputtered impurities to the divertor region.
AI.conclusion: The low-recycling regime is viable for improving tokamak performance by maximizing energy confinement while also addressing reactor damage concerns through kinetic effects that reduce heat flux and prevent core contamination.

title: High-order, long-time stable and parallel decoupled GBDF$k$ SAV ensemble schemes for the Navier--Stokes--Darcy flow with random hydraulic conductivity tensors
summary: We develop and analyze high-order ensemble schemes for the unsteady Navier--Stokes--Darcy system with uncertain initial conditions, forcing terms, hydraulic conductivity tensors, and Lions-Beavers-Joseph-Saffman interface conditions. The proposed schemes which are called GSAV-GBDF$k$-Ensemble schemes integrate a partitioned decoupling strategy, the generalized scalar auxiliary variable (GSAV) approach, and generalized BDF$k$ discretizations. This framework achieves high-order temporal accuracy and long-time stability, permits explicit treatment of the nonlinear term, and facilitates an efficient ensemble implementation for multiple parameter realizations by sharing a single, unified coefficient matrix at each time step. Moreover, the numerical solutions are shown to satisfy uniform-in-time bounds without time-step restrictions. Owing to the ensemble formulation, the resulting linear systems share common coefficient matrices, which significantly improves computational efficiency. We further establish optimal-order error estimates for the proposed high-order schemes. Numerical results are included to confirm the theoretical analysis and to illustrate the accuracy, stability, and efficiency of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces high-order ensemble schemes (GSAV-GBDFk-Ensemble) for the Navier-Stokes-Darcy system with uncertain parameters, achieving high-order temporal accuracy, long-time stability, and computational efficiency through shared coefficient matrices.
AI.motivation: To address the computational challenges of simulating unsteady Navier-Stokes-Darcy systems under multiple sources of uncertainty (initial conditions, forcing terms, hydraulic conductivity tensors, and interface conditions) by developing efficient ensemble methods.
AI.method: The proposed GSAV-GBDFk-Ensemble schemes combine three key components: a partitioned decoupling strategy, the generalized scalar auxiliary variable (GSAV) approach, and generalized BDFk discretizations. This enables explicit treatment of nonlinear terms and shared coefficient matrices across realizations.
AI.result: The schemes achieve high-order temporal accuracy, long-time stability with uniform-in-time bounds without time-step restrictions, optimal-order error estimates, and significant computational efficiency gains through shared linear system coefficient matrices. Numerical results confirm the theoretical analysis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ensemble framework provides an accurate, stable, and efficient approach for solving Navier-Stokes-Darcy systems with uncertainty, with rigorously proven optimal error estimates and demonstrated computational advantages.

title: Low-regularity well-posedness for the ZK equation on a half-strip
summary: Studied here is the Zakharov--Kuznetsov equation with a linear transport term posed on a half-strip with nonhomogeneous boundary condition. Using Bourgain-type spaces adapted to the ZK dispersive structure, anisotropic smoothing and boundary trace estimates, we establish its local well-posedness in $L^2.$

AI.tldr: This paper studies the Zakharov-Kuznetsov equation with a linear transport term on a half-strip with nonhomogeneous boundary conditions, proving local well-posedness in L² using Bourgain-type spaces and anisotropic smoothing estimates.
AI.motivation: To establish well-posedness for the ZK equation in a bounded domain (half-strip) with nonhomogeneous boundary conditions, which is physically relevant for plasma physics and requires specialized analytical tools.
AI.method: The authors employ Bourgain-type spaces adapted to the ZK dispersive structure, combined with anisotropic smoothing and boundary trace estimates to handle the nonhomogeneous boundary condition.
AI.result: Local well-posedness in L² is established for the ZK equation with a linear transport term on a half-strip with nonhomogeneous boundary condition.
AI.conclusion: The adapted Bourgain space approach successfully yields well-posedness results for this boundary value problem.

title: Emergent heavy-tailed distributions from a Markovian random walk
summary: The emergence of heavy-tailed statistics in complex systems is conventionally attributed to non-local stochastic jumps or non-Markovian memory. Here, we present a one-dimensional random walk where power-law behaviors arise instead from a strictly local, discrete-time Markovian mechanism. The step length is governed by a deterministic function of the walker's position, establishing a positive feedback loop that induces strong effective correlations along the trajectories. Through analytical derivations in the continuum limit and extensive numerical simulations, we show that this rule yields a robust, non-Gaussian stationary state. The exact analytical solution is obtained in the closed form of a symmetric, Lorentz-like distribution, $ρ_{\text{st}}(x) \propto (|x|/l+rΔx)^{-2}$, confirming asymptotic power-law tails that decay as $|x|^{-2}$ over six decades. Furthermore, by employing the Onsager-Machlup path-integral formalism, we demonstrate that effective velocity and acceleration acquire physical meaning along the shortest fluctuation trajectories. Crucially, we find that a non-zero initial acceleration acts as the fundamental mechanism driving the walker away from the origin, ensuring both the emergence of scale-free statistics and the normalizability of the stationary distribution. This minimal pathway provides a new microscopic foundation for the widespread $-2$ power law observed across multidisciplinary complex systems.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that heavy-tailed power-law statistics can emerge from a simple one-dimensional random walk with a local, Markovian, deterministic step-length rule, yielding a stationary Lorentz-like distribution with |x|⁻² tails.
AI.motivation: To challenge the conventional view that power-law behaviors in complex systems require non-local jumps or non-Markovian memory, and to provide a minimal microscopic mechanism for the widespread -2 power law.
AI.method: A one-dimensional discrete-time random walk where step length is a deterministic function of the walker's position, creating positive feedback. Analytical continuum-limit derivations, numerical simulations over six decades, and Onsager-Machlup path-integral formalism are employed.
AI.result: The stationary distribution is a symmetric Lorentz-like form ρ_st(x) ∝ (|x|/l + rΔx)⁻², confirming asymptotic power-law tails decaying as |x|⁻². Non-zero initial acceleration is identified as the key mechanism driving the walker from the origin.
AI.conclusion: A minimal, purely local Markovian pathway can produce scale-free heavy-tailed statistics, offering a new microscopic foundation for the -2 power law observed across multidisciplinary complex systems.

title: First-Principles Explanation of the Drift Configuration Dependence of the Radial Electric Field and High-Confinement Access in Tokamaks
summary: The origin of the difference in the high-confinement (H-mode) power threshold between favorable and unfavorable drift configurations in tokamaks, experimentally linked to a deeper radial electric field (Er) well in the former, remains unresolved. Using first-principles gyrokinetic simulations of edge and scrape-off-layer turbulence in ASDEX Upgrade, we show that turbulence-driven poloidal flows generate this deeper Er well in the favorable configuration through enhanced nonlinear turbulence-mean flow energy transfer. This transfer is significantly weaker in the unfavorable case, yielding a shallower Er well, while turbulence intensity is simultaneously higher. Within the turbulence-flow shear suppression paradigm, the combination of stronger shear and reduced turbulence facilitates H-mode access in the favorable configuration. These results provide the first validated, self-consistent full-f gyrokinetic explanation of how drift configuration controls the nonlinear dynamics of profiles, Er, flows, and turbulence, thereby setting the H-mode power threshold.

AI.tldr: Using gyrokinetic simulations, the study explains why the favorable drift configuration in tokamaks has a lower H-mode power threshold: turbulence-driven poloidal flows create a deeper radial electric field well via stronger nonlinear energy transfer, leading to better turbulence suppression and easier H-mode access.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing mystery of why the H-mode power threshold differs between favorable and unfavorable drift configurations in tokamaks, specifically the deeper radial electric field well observed in the favorable case.
AI.method: First-principles gyrokinetic simulations of edge and scrape-off-layer turbulence in ASDEX Upgrade tokamak, using full-f framework to self-consistently capture profile, flow, and turbulence dynamics.
AI.result: The favorable drift configuration exhibits stronger nonlinear turbulence-mean flow energy transfer, producing a deeper Er well with stronger shear and lower turbulence intensity. The unfavorable case shows weaker transfer, a shallower Er well, and higher turbulence.
AI.conclusion: Drift configuration controls H-mode power threshold via nonlinear turbulence-flow shear suppression dynamics, providing a validated gyrokinetic explanation for this key fusion confinement phenomenon.

title: Geometric properties of the Lebesgue function
summary: We present a collection of observations concerning the peculiar behavior of the Lebesgue function in the setting of the interval $[-1,1]\subset \mathbb{R}$ and the square $[-1,1]^2\subset \mathbb{R}^2$. We provide numerical results and formulate several open problems related to the geometry of the Lebesgue function.

AI.tldr: The paper presents observations, numerical results, and open problems about the Lebesgue function's unusual behavior on the interval [-1,1] and square [-1,1]², focusing on its geometric properties.
AI.motivation: To investigate and document peculiar behaviors of the Lebesgue function in one-dimensional and two-dimensional settings, and to stimulate further research by posing open problems.
AI.method: The study employs numerical computations and geometric analysis to examine the Lebesgue function's behavior on [-1,1] and [-1,1]², combined with theoretical observations.
AI.result: Numerical results are presented that reveal unusual characteristics of the Lebesgue function in both the interval and square domains.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes with several open problems concerning the geometry of the Lebesgue function, inviting further investigation.

title: A Mountain-Pass Algorithm for Nonlocal Problems with Super-quadratic Nonlinearities
summary:
In this paper we consider a nonlinear equation $-\mathcal{L} u(x) = f(x, u(x))$ with a super-quadratic nonlinearity, $f$, and a nonlocal operator, $\mathcal{L}$, generated by a special class of radially symmetric $L^1$ convolution kernels with finite second moments. The assumptions on this operator are mild and allow for a variety of kernels used in biological and physical applications, including kernels with algebraic decay and sign changing kernels.
  Using the strong nonlinearities present in the equation, we prove the existence of nontrivial solutions using the classical Mountain Pass Theorem, a central result in minimax theory that equates solutions of our equation to critical points of a corresponding energy functional. This existence result holds with both homogeneous nonlocal Dirichlet and nonlocal Neumann boundary conditions. We supplement these theoretical results with numerical simulations for various nonlinearities with odd maximal degree in the unknown $u$. The numerical scheme exploits the resulting energy landscape which allows one to adapt a gradient descent algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the existence of nontrivial solutions for a nonlinear nonlocal equation with super-quadratic nonlinearity using the Mountain Pass Theorem, and provides numerical simulations via gradient descent on the energy landscape.
AI.motivation: To establish existence of nontrivial solutions for a broad class of nonlocal operators with super-quadratic nonlinearities, accommodating various kernel types used in biological and physical applications (e.g., algebraically decaying and sign-changing kernels).
AI.method: Classical Mountain Pass Theorem applied to a corresponding energy functional, with both nonlocal Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions; numerical simulation via gradient descent on the energy landscape.
AI.result: Existence of nontrivial solutions proven under mild assumptions on the kernel; numerical simulations confirm results for various odd-maximal-degree nonlinearities.
AI.conclusion: The Mountain Pass Theorem is effective for establishing solutions in this nonlocal setting, and the energy-based numerical scheme is viable for practical computation.

title: Absorbing Many-Body Correlations into Core-Optimized Orbitals
summary: The cost of simulating quantum many-body systems - on classical or quantum hardware - scales with the number of variational parameters, so progress at fixed computational budget hinges on more parameter-efficient ansätze. Configuration Interaction (CI) is widely dismissed as parameter-heavy; we show this verdict is an artifact of the orbital basis. Co-optimizing the orbital basis with a sparse CI wavefunction - a method we call Core-Optimized Orbitals (COO) - absorbs a large fraction of the dynamical correlation directly into the single-particle basis, cutting the determinant count by several orders of magnitude beyond the already compact TrimCI ansatz on which it builds. On [Fe$_4$S$_4$] (54e, 36o), a billion-determinant TrimCI+COO wavefunction reaches accuracy that would require $3\!\times\!10^{14}$ determinants in a localized basis. At matched accuracy, it is $8\times$ more compact than the largest unrestricted-DMRG benchmark ($25\times$ with PT2). Across the iron-sulfur series - from [Fe$_2$S$_2$] (30e,20o) to the P-cluster (114e,73o) - TrimCI+COO is $10$-$100\times$ more compact than SU(2)-adapted DMRG with entanglement-minimized orbitals at matched accuracy. A tunable Hubbard-on-graph model factorizes the advantage into an orbital-basis gain and an ansatz gain, the latter capturing multi-center entanglement that resists MPS localization. COO therefore changes the picture of CI efficiency: sparse CI with optimized orbitals can outperform state-of-the-art tensor networks on strongly correlated multi-center systems.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Configuration Interaction (CI) can be made dramatically more efficient by co-optimizing orbitals with sparse CI wavefunctions (COO method). On iron-sulfur clusters, this approach achieves 10-100× more compact wavefunctions than DMRG, challenging the conventional wisdom that CI is parameter-heavy.
AI.motivation: The cost of simulating quantum many-body systems scales with variational parameters, so more parameter-efficient ansätze are needed. Configuration Interaction (CI) has been widely dismissed as parameter-heavy, but the authors hypothesize this is an artifact of the orbital basis choice.
AI.method: The method, called Core-Optimized Orbitals (COO), co-optimizes the orbital basis with a sparse CI wavefunction. It builds on the TrimCI ansatz and absorbs dynamical correlation into the single-particle basis, drastically reducing determinant count.
AI.result: On [Fe₄S₄] (54e,36o), a billion-determinant TrimCI+COO wavefunction achieves accuracy requiring 3×10¹⁴ determinants in a localized basis. It is 8× more compact than unrestricted-DMRG (25× with PT2). Across iron-sulfur clusters, TrimCI+COO is 10-100× more compact than SU(2)-adapted DMRG.
AI.conclusion: COO changes the picture of CI efficiency: sparse CI with optimized orbitals can outperform state-of-the-art tensor networks on strongly correlated multi-center systems.

title: Pre L-H Transition Radial Electric Field and Transport Validations of Edge and Scrape-off Layer Gyrokinetic Simulations at ASDEX Upgrade
summary: This work presents a stepwise validation of the evolution of the radial electric field (Er) and heat transport during the pre L-H transition phase using full-f gyrokinetic simulations of the edge and scrape-off layer in the ASDEX Upgrade (AUG) tokamak, including X-point geometry. Several L-mode time slices up to the L-H transition from a dedicated hydrogen discharge, featuring stepwise increases in ECRH input power, are selected [N. Bonanomi \textit{et al.}, Phys. Plasmas 31, 072302 (2024)] and simulated with the \texttt{GENE-X} code. As the edge boundary conditions are progressively increased between the time slices, particle and heat fluxes rise, and the radial electric field Er well deepens. A detailed validation of the Er profiles and of the Er well depth shows excellent agreement with experimental measurements at the successive time slices approaching the L-H transition. A force balance decomposition identifies turbulence-driven poloidal flows as the dominant contribution within the Er well. Edge turbulence is governed by a competition between electron drift waves and trapped-electron modes. The introduction of an edge density source, modeling neutral gas ionization, is shown to be essential to reproduce experimentally relevant density profiles, Er, and edge ion heat fluxes, which are dominated by both turbulent and diamagnetic contributions. This stepwise validation constitutes an important milestone toward predictive, first-principles gyrokinetic simulations of the L-H transition power threshold.

AI.tldr: This paper validates gyrokinetic simulations of radial electric field and heat transport during pre-L-H transition in AUG tokamak, showing excellent agreement with experiments. Turbulence-driven poloidal flows dominate the Er well, and edge density sources are crucial for reproducing experimental profiles.
AI.motivation: To achieve predictive, first-principles gyrokinetic simulations of the L-H transition power threshold by validating the evolution of radial electric field and heat transport during the pre-L-H transition phase.
AI.method: Full-f gyrokinetic simulations using GENE-X code for edge & scrape-off layer of AUG tokamak with X-point geometry. Several L-mode time slices with stepwise ECRH power increases were simulated, including force balance decomposition and edge density source analysis.
AI.result: Excellent agreement between simulated and experimental Er profiles. Turbulence-driven poloidal flows dominate Er well formation. Edge turbulence governed by competition between drift waves and trapped-electron modes. Edge density source essential for realistic density, Er, and heat flux profiles.
AI.conclusion: This stepwise validation marks an important milestone toward predictive gyrokinetic simulations of the L-H transition power threshold.

title: Nonconforming Finite Element Approximation and Energy Lower Bound Estimation for the Gross--Pitaevskii Energy Functional
summary: The ground state of Bose--Einstein condensates can be described as the minimizer of the Gross--Pitaevskii energy functional subject to a mass conservation constraint. In this paper, we study the corresponding discrete optimization problem in nonconforming finite element spaces and establish a priori error estimates for the discrete ground state energy, the discrete eigenvalue, and the discrete ground state. Specifically, we derive explicit convergence rates for the a priori error in the particular case of the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ finite element. Furthermore, we proof that within the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ finite element framework, the discrete ground state energy provides a lower bound estimation to the exact energy. Finally, numerical experiments are presented to validate the theoretical analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a priori error estimates for computing ground states of Bose-Einstein condensates using nonconforming finite elements, showing that the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ element yields lower bounds for the exact energy with explicit convergence rates, validated by numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous a priori error estimates for discrete ground state energy, eigenvalue, and ground state when solving the Gross-Pitaevskii eigenvalue problem using nonconforming finite element methods, particularly for the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ element.
AI.method: The paper studies the discrete optimization problem in nonconforming finite element spaces, derives explicit convergence rates for a priori errors for the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ element, and proves that the discrete ground state energy provides a lower bound to the exact energy.
AI.result: Explicit convergence rates for a priori errors are derived, and it is proven that the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ finite element framework yields a lower bound estimation for the exact ground state energy.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical analysis is validated by numerical experiments, confirming the effectiveness of the $EQ_1^{\mathrm{rot}}$ nonconforming finite element for ground state computation of Bose-Einstein condensates.

title: Symmetry and classification of positive standing waves of nonlinear Hartree type equations
summary:
This paper presents some qualitative properties of positive solutions to the strongly coupled system \[ \begin{cases} \displaystyle - Δu + τu =
\frac{2 p}{p + q} \left( I_α\ast |v|^q \right) |u|^{p - 2} u &\text{in} ~ \mathbb{R}^N, \\ \\ \displaystyle - Δv + ηv =
\frac{2 q}{p + q} \left( I_α\ast |u|^p \right) |v|^{q - 2} v &\text{in} ~ \mathbb{R}^N, \end{cases} \] with $τ, η> 0$, $N \in \mathbb{N}$, $0 < α< N$, \[ \max \left\{1, \frac{2 α}{N}\right\} < p, q < 2^* \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{2 (N + α)}{N} < p + q < 2_α^*, \] where $I_α$ denotes the Riesz potential, \[ 2^* := \begin{cases} \infty, &\text{if} ~ N \in \{1, 2\}, \\ \frac{2 N}{N - 2}, &\text{if} ~ N \geq 3, \end{cases} \quad \text{and} \quad 2_α^* := \begin{cases} \infty, &\text{if} ~ N \in \{1, 2\}, \\ \frac{2 (N + α)}{N - 2}, &\text{if} ~ N \geq 3. \end{cases} \] More precisely, by means of the moving planes method, we prove that positive solutions to this system are radially symmetric and strictly radially decreasing when $p, q \geq 2$, and we obtain a classification result for positive ground states in the case $p = q$ and $τ= η$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies positive solutions of a strongly coupled system with Riesz potentials, proving radial symmetry and strict monotonicity for positive solutions when p,q≥2, and a classification result for ground states when p=q and τ=η.
AI.motivation: To understand qualitative properties (symmetry, monotonicity, classification) of positive solutions to a strongly coupled elliptic system involving Riesz potentials, which is important for analyzing nonlocal interactions in mathematical physics.
AI.method: Moving planes method is employed to establish radial symmetry and strict radial decreasing of positive solutions under given parameter conditions.
AI.result: Positive solutions are radially symmetric and strictly radially decreasing when p,q≥2. A classification result for positive ground states is obtained in the special case p=q and τ=η.
AI.conclusion: The moving planes method can be effectively applied to this strongly coupled system with Riesz potentials to deduce symmetry properties and classification of ground states.

title: Weakly nonlinear interaction of capillary waves in a finite system: leading interaction process and scales' range of direct energy cascade
summary: During comprehensive study of weakly nonlinear interaction of surface capillary waves, processes of resonant and non-resonant interactions were considered both numerically and analytically: merging of two waves into one and waves on the ring (in Fourier space, isotropic spectrum) into larger diameter ring. It was shown numerically, that these resonant processes are the leading ones and other processes with respect to them are at least weaker if manifest themselves at all. It was confirmed, that resonant the processes are the major ones which contribute to the long time dynamics. In the case of isotropic turbulence of capillary waves the formation of wave turbulence's Zakharov-Filonenko spectrum is demonstrated. It was also shown, that this spectrum in finite systems has a finite range of scales. Due to finiteness of the numerical simulation or experimental area the discreteness of the wavenumbers grid arrest local in Fourier space resonant interaction when smaller scales are considered. Scaling of the range of realization of the Zakharov-Filonenko spectrum, depending on main parameters of the numerical or experimental setup (average steepness and characteristic size), is derived analytically and partially confirmed numerically.

AI.tldr: This paper studies weakly nonlinear interactions of surface capillary waves, showing that resonant processes (merging two waves into one, wave ring interactions) dominate over non-resonant ones in long-time dynamics. It demonstrates the formation of the Zakharov-Filonenko spectrum for isotropic capillary wave turbulence, but finite system size limits the spectrum's range due to discreteness of wavenumber grid arresting resonant interactions at small scales. Analytical scaling for the spectrum's range dependent on average steepness and system size is derived and partially confirmed numerically.
AI.motivation: To identify which types of nonlinear interactions (resonant vs. non-resonant) are dominant in capillary wave turbulence, and to understand how finite system size affects the formation of the theoretically predicted Zakharov-Filonenko spectrum.
AI.method: Combined numerical simulation and analytical study of weakly nonlinear capillary wave interactions, examining both discrete wave merging processes and isotropic ring interactions in Fourier space.
AI.result: Resonant processes (two-wave merging and ring interactions) are the leading mechanisms, while non-resonant processes are at least weaker. The Zakharov-Filonenko spectrum forms but has a finite range limited by the discreteness of the wavenumber grid at small scales. An analytical scaling law for the spectrum's range based on average steepness and system size was derived.
AI.conclusion: Resonant interactions govern long-time capillary wave dynamics, but finite simulation/experimental domains impose a scale cutoff on the wave turbulence spectrum due to discretization effects, with the accessible range scaling predictably with system parameters.

title: NIMROD-to-IMAS workflow for extended-magnetohydrodynamic data with reusable datasets and implications for IMAS schema development
summary: Extended magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations of tokamak plasmas regularly produce outputs in multi-dimensional, multiple-field formats; these code-specific formats make it difficult to do cross-code validation/coupling and analyze at a database scale. In this paper, a workflow that converts NIMROD code inputs and outputs to records compatible with version 4 of the ITER IMAS Data Dictionary is presented. The scope of the workflow includes preprocessing of NIMROD code inputs, conversion of hierarchical NIMROD code HDF5 dumps, COCOS-consistent treatment of the coordinate system and sign convention, and encoding finite-element poloidal meshes and toroidal Fourier components through IMAS General Grid Description. Furthermore, the workflow allows for provenance and integrity metadata to be included while providing optimal I/O operations for large array structures. An example conversion based on an NIMROD code simulation of edge harmonic oscillations performed for the DIII-D discharge 163518 [A.Y. Pankin, et al., Nuclear Fusion 60.9 (2020), p. 092004] is used to validate the conservation of essential equilibrium, profile, perturbation, and grid data in the resulting IMAS records. Finally, this implementation exposes gaps in the current IMAS framework that need to be addressed to accommodate extended MHD data, and it highlights the metadata, provenance, and governance needs of the downstream use cases in the form of dataset validation, integration, and machine learning.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a workflow to convert NIMROD MHD simulation data into the standardized IMAS format (v4), enabling cross-code validation and database-scale analysis. It addresses coordinate systems, mesh encoding, metadata provenance, and validates the approach using DIII-D discharge data, while identifying gaps in the current IMAS framework for extended MHD data.
AI.motivation: Multi-dimensional, multi-field MHD simulation outputs are in code-specific formats, hindering cross-code validation, coupling, and large-scale database analysis. A standardized data format is needed.
AI.method: A workflow is developed to convert NIMROD inputs/outputs into IMAS Data Dictionary v4 compatible records. It includes preprocessing, hierarchical HDF5 conversion, COCOS-consistent coordinate/sign convention handling, and finite-element mesh/toroidal Fourier encoding via IMAS General Grid Description.
AI.result: The workflow successfully converts NIMROD data to IMAS format, validated using DIII-D discharge 163518 edge harmonic oscillation simulations. Essential equilibrium, profile, perturbation, and grid data are preserved. Gaps in the current IMAS framework for extended MHD data are identified.
AI.conclusion: The workflow demonstrates feasibility of standardizing extended MHD data, but reveals limitations in IMAS that require addressing for broader adoption. Downstream needs for dataset validation, integration, and ML are highlighted.

title: Key challenges and bridges among convergence analysis techniques for polytopal methods
summary: Polytopal methods provide a flexible framework for the numerical approximation of partial differential equations on general meshes. Their convergence analysis raises specific challenges due to their inherently non-conforming nature and, in many cases, the fully discrete nature of their solution. Two main techniques are considered: the virtual-function approach, used, e.g., in the context of Virtual Element Methods, and the fully discrete approach, which underlies, e.g., the Discrete de Rham method. We introduce here a novel framework based on the notion of conforming liftings, namely bounded and consistent mappings from the discrete space into the continuous space. This approach bridges the virtual and fully discrete viewpoints, clarifies the role of norm equivalence for virtual functions, and leads to a decomposition of the consistency error usable for polytopal methods. The three approaches are demonstrated on a model problem, which provides the opportunity to discuss relevant technical points. Bridges with the convergence properties of discrete differential complexes are also built.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel conforming lifting framework that unifies virtual-function and fully discrete approaches for convergence analysis of polytopal methods, demonstrated on a model problem with connections to discrete differential complexes.
AI.motivation: Polytopal methods (e.g., VEM and DDR) face convergence analysis challenges due to their non-conforming and fully discrete nature; existing virtual-function and fully discrete approaches lack a unified theoretical framework.
AI.method: A novel framework based on conforming liftings (bounded, consistent mappings from discrete to continuous spaces) is introduced to bridge virtual and fully discrete viewpoints, enabling decomposition of consistency error.
AI.result: The framework clarifies norm equivalence for virtual functions, provides decomposition of consistency error usable for polytopal methods, and builds bridges with discrete differential complexes.
AI.conclusion: The conforming lifting approach successfully unifies existing analysis techniques and demonstrates applicability to a model problem, offering a flexible tool for polytopal method convergence analysis.

title: Spatial decay and nonlinear smoothing of the generalized Ostrovsky equation
summary: This paper is devoted to studying the generalized Ostrovsky equation \begin{eqnarray*} u_{t}-β\partial_{x}^{3}u-γ\partial_{x}^{-1}u+\frac{1}{k+1}(u^{k+1})_{x}=0,k\geq5 \end{eqnarray*} with $β<0,γ>0$. Firstly, by using the density theorem in the mixed Lebesgue spaces, we prove that $X_{s,b}\hookrightarrow C(\mathbb{R};H^{s}(\mathbb{R})) \hookrightarrow C(\mathbb{R};L_{x}^{\infty})$ with $s>1/2,b>1/2.$ Secondly, we present a new proof of the convergence problem of linear Ostrovsky equation, which is slightly different from the proof of Theorem 1.1 (Convergence problem of Ostrovsky equation with rough data and random data, Indiana Univ. Math. J. 71(2022), 1897-1921.) Thirdly, we investigate the pointwise convergence problem of the generalized Ostrovsky equation. Fourthly, for the solution $u$ to the Cauchy problem for the generalized Ostrovsky equation, we prove that $u=u_{1}+u_{2},t\in[-δ,δ]$, and $u_{2}$ possesses better regularity than $u$, where $u_{1}$ is the linear part of $u$ and $u_{2}$ is the nonlinear integral part. Fifthly, we investigate the nonlinear smoothing and the uniform convergence problem of the generalized Ostrovsky equation. Finally, when data $f$ belongs to $H^{s}(\mathbb{R})(s>\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{k+1},k\geq6)$ and $\lim\limits_{|x|\rightarrow{\infty}}f=0$ and $\mathscr{F}_{x}(U(t)f)\in L^{1}(\mathbb{R}),$ for $t\in [-δ,δ],$ we prove that $\lim\limits_{|x|\rightarrow{\infty}}u=0$. The key ingredients are high-low frequency technique, maximal function estimates related to low frequency and some Strichartz estimates which can be proved with the aid of the Stein complex interpolation Theorem.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the generalized Ostrovsky equation (k≥5) with β<0,γ>0, proving embedding properties, convergence problems, pointwise convergence, nonlinear smoothing, uniform convergence, and decay at infinity using high-low frequency techniques, maximal function estimates, and Strichartz estimates via Stein complex interpolation.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of the generalized Ostrovsky equation beyond existing results, particularly for higher nonlinearity (k≥5), and to provide new proofs for convergence problems and pointwise convergence behavior.
AI.method: Uses density theorems in mixed Lebesgue spaces, high-low frequency technique, maximal function estimates for low frequency, Strichartz estimates proved via Stein complex interpolation theorem, and decomposition of solutions into linear and nonlinear parts.
AI.result: (1) X_{s,b} embeddings into C(R;H^s(R)) and C(R;L_x^∞) for s>1/2,b>1/2. (2) New proof of linear Ostrovsky equation convergence. (3) Pointwise convergence for generalized equation. (4) Solution decomposition u=u₁+u₂ with u₂ having better regularity. (5) Nonlinear smoothing and uniform convergence results. (6) Decay at infinity (lim_{|x|→∞} u=0) for data f∈H^s(R) with s>1/2-2/(k+1), k≥6.
AI.conclusion: The paper systematically establishes well-posedness, regularity, convergence, and asymptotic decay properties for the generalized Ostrovsky equation with high-order nonlinearities, contributing new theoretical tools and proofs to the field.

title: The two-stream instability generation around Moon: Effect of Interplanetary Magnetic Field during Solar Wind - Lunar Plasma Interaction
summary: The relative motion of two interpenetrating streams of charged particles usually leads to the generation of two stream instability (TSI) and eventually result in the onset of non-linear plasma processes such as the turbulence or the plasma waves. A natural example of such an event is the solar wind interaction with the lunar electron plasma where the interplanetary magnetic field (IMF) is embedded within the solar wind. The inclusion of IMF in the solar wind lunar plasma interaction modifies the dispersion relation of the TSI and an angular cyclotron frequency term appears in the denominator of the leading term and hence leads to the change in the parameters such as the instability growth rate as now it depends on the solar wind electron velocity, solar wind and the lunar electron plasma density, and the IMF magnitude. It is observed that the growth rate increases fast with the increase in the magnetic field initially but the increase slows down on further increasing the magnetic field thereby smoothening the top. From the particle in cell (PIC) simulations, it is observed that during the solar wind IMF interaction with lunar plasma, the non-energetic background electrons make a shield around the solar wind electrons in the vortices formed. It is further observed that those lunar electrons which are not participating either in the vortices formation or in the shielding of solar wind electrons, start moving in the direction of the incoming solar wind. These observations indicate this interaction is capable in getting converted into a non-linear physical process in the lunar plasma environment.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how the interplanetary magnetic field (IMF) modifies the two-stream instability (TSI) in solar wind-lunar plasma interactions, showing that the growth rate initially increases rapidly with magnetic field strength then plateaus. PIC simulations reveal electron shielding and vortex formation, indicating transition to nonlinear processes.
AI.motivation: To understand how the interplanetary magnetic field (IMF) modifies the two-stream instability (TSI) in the context of solar wind interacting with lunar electron plasma, and to characterize the nonlinear plasma processes that emerge from this interaction.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of the modified TSI dispersion relation including IMF effects, combined with Particle-in-Cell (PIC) simulations to observe electron dynamics, vortex formation, and shielding behavior during solar wind-lunar plasma interaction.
AI.result: The growth rate increases sharply with magnetic field initially but slows down at higher fields, flattening the curve. PIC simulations show background lunar electrons shielding solar wind electrons within vortices, while non-participating lunar electrons drift toward the incoming solar wind.
AI.conclusion: The solar wind-lunar plasma interaction with embedded IMF can transition into nonlinear physical processes in the lunar plasma environment, driven by the modified TSI and electron dynamics observed in simulations.

title: A high-order nodally bound-preserving and mass-conservative method for linear fourth-order elliptic problems and its applications to nonlinear parabolic equations
summary:
We propose a high-order finite element method for linear fourth-order elliptic problems that is both nodally bound-preserving and mass-conservative, based on a variational inequality formulation. The method admits an equivalent strictly convex minimization structure, which ensures well-posedness and enables an optimal error estimate in the $H^1$-seminorm, under suitable regularity assumptions.
  This framework is further extended to nonlinear fourth-order parabolic problems through space--time high-order discretizations that combine variational inequalities, BDF schemes, and scalar auxiliary variable (SAV) techniques. The fully discrete schemes preserve nodal bounds and mass, and a modified energy stability result is established for the first-order temporal scheme. We also apply the same framework to nonlinear second-order parabolic problems by introducing a consistent fourth-order regularization, leading to space--time high-order schemes with the same bound-preserving and mass-conservative properties.
  Extensive numerical results, including challenging tests with singularities and low regularity, demonstrate the stability, efficiency, and high-order accuracy of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: A high-order finite element method preserving nodal bounds and mass conservation is proposed for linear fourth-order elliptic, nonlinear fourth-order parabolic, and nonlinear second-order parabolic problems, using variational inequalities, BDF schemes, and SAV techniques, with theoretical guarantees and extensive numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To develop numerical methods for fourth-order elliptic/parabolic problems that simultaneously satisfy two critical physical/geometric constraints: nodal bound preservation and mass conservation, while achieving high-order accuracy.
AI.method: High-order finite element method with variational inequality formulation (elliptic); extended to parabolic via space-time discretizations combining variational inequalities, BDF schemes, and SAV techniques; also applied to second-order parabolic problems via fourth-order regularization.
AI.result: Optimal error estimate in H1-seminorm for elliptic case; modified energy stability for first-order temporal scheme; extensive numerical tests including singular/low-regularity scenarios demonstrate stability, efficiency, and high-order accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enforces both bound preservation and mass conservation for a wide class of problems (elliptic & parabolic, linear & nonlinear, fourth-order & second-order) while maintaining high-order accuracy and stability.

title: On the Tangential Traces of Curl-Measure Fields
summary: Curl-measure fields are $p$-integrable vector fields whose distributional curl is a vector-valued Radon measure with finite total variation. They were introduced in arXiv:2509.26465, where, for $p= \infty$, the existence of tangential traces for bounded Lipschitz domains was established, together with the tangential property of the trace. In this paper, we show that the same tangential property holds for domains that are sets of finite perimeter.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the tangential property of curl-measure fields from bounded Lipschitz domains to sets of finite perimeter.
AI.motivation: To generalize the tangential trace property of curl-measure fields, previously proven only for bounded Lipschitz domains, to a broader class of domains (sets of finite perimeter).
AI.method: The authors analyze curl-measure fields (p-integrable vector fields with distributional curl as a vector-valued Radon measure) and extend the trace results from Lipschitz domains to sets of finite perimeter.
AI.result: The tangential property of the trace holds for domains that are sets of finite perimeter, not just bounded Lipschitz domains.
AI.conclusion: The tangential trace property for curl-measure fields is valid on a wider class of domains (sets of finite perimeter), generalizing previous results.

title: Intrinsic Point Defects and Frenkel Pair Formation in Photovoltaic Absorber Zn$_3$P$_2$: Regulating $p$-type Conductivity through Growth and Annealing Conditions
summary: This study investigates the ground-state energetics and thermodynamics of intrinsic point defects in zinc phosphide Zn$_3$P$_2$ using \emph{ab initio} density functional theory combined with an extensive potential energy landscape search. Our analysis reveals that the defect chemistry is dominated by zinc vacancies $V_\mathrm{Zn}$ and zinc interstitials Zn$_i$, with equilibrium concentrations significantly surpassing those of other intrinsic species. Notably, we find that phosphorus interstitials P$_i$, previously suggested to be significant, possess high formation energies and likely exist only in negligible quantities. The characteristic $p$-type conductivity of undoped Zn$_3$P$_2$ is shown to be a direct consequence of zinc vacancies, which act as shallow acceptors and pull the Fermi level toward the valence band. Furthermore, we identify a positive binding energy between $V_\mathrm{Zn}$ and Zn$_i$, leading to the formation of electrically benign Frenkel pairs that partially compensate the intrinsic p-type conductivity. Our results suggest that achieving $n$-type conductivity is fundamentally limited by these thermodynamic constraints. We conclude that hole densities can be optimized through phosphorus-rich growth conditions and high-temperature annealing, and suggest that future photovoltaic strategies should prioritize interface engineering over bulk $n$-type doping.

AI.tldr: DFT study shows Zn vacancies dominate p-type conductivity in Zn₃P₂, while P interstitials are negligible; Frenkel pairs partially compensate conductivity, limiting n-type doping.
AI.motivation: To understand the defect chemistry and origin of p-type conductivity in Zn₃P₂, and assess feasibility of n-type doping for photovoltaic applications.
AI.method: Ab initio density functional theory combined with extensive potential energy landscape search.
AI.result: Zn vacancies and interstitials dominate; P interstitials have high formation energies; V_Zn acts as shallow acceptors; Frenkel pairs with positive binding energy partially compensate p-type.
AI.conclusion: Hole density can be optimized via P-rich growth and annealing; n-type doping is fundamentally limited; focus should shift to interface engineering.

title: A quasi-neutral electromagnetic hybrid model with drift-kinetic electrons and fully kinetic ions
summary: In this work, we propose a hybrid model that combines drift-kinetic electrons with fully kinetic ions under the quasi-neutrality assumption, discretized using a geometric particle-in-cell framework on dual-grids. The model advances the perturbed electromagnetic fields $E$ and $B$ directly, rather than the scalar and vector potentials. The parallel electric field $E_\parallel$ is obtained from Ohm's law. The perpendicular electric field $E_\perp$ is computed from Ampère's law by extracting the $E_\perp$-dependent component of the drift-kinetic electron current. The quasi-neutrality constraint eliminates high-frequency light waves and Langmuir waves from the system. Temporal discretization is performed using low-storage Runge--Kutta schemes. In this quasi-neutral hybrid model, the right-hand polarized wave branch exhibits a whistler-like dispersion relation, which imposes a stringent timestep constraint. To address this, we develop a novel implicit-explicit splitting scheme for Faraday's law that significantly relaxes the timestep stability restriction. The model is validated in slab geometry by reproducing cold plasma wave branches, ion Bernstein waves, compressional and shear Alfvén waves, and ion acoustic waves.

AI.tldr: A hybrid drift-kinetic electron / fully kinetic ion model using geometric particle-in-cell on dual-grids is proposed, with an implicit-explicit splitting scheme for Faraday's law that relaxes the timestep constraint, validated on various wave branches.
AI.motivation: To develop a computationally efficient quasi-neutral hybrid model that eliminates high-frequency waves (light waves, Langmuir waves) while accurately capturing low-frequency plasma dynamics, and to overcome the stringent timestep constraint imposed by the whistler-like dispersion relation.
AI.method: Combines drift-kinetic electrons with fully kinetic ions under quasi-neutrality; uses geometric particle-in-cell on dual-grids; advances perturbed E and B fields directly; obtains E_parallel from Ohm's law and E_perp from Ampère's law using drift-kinetic electron current; employs low-storage Runge-Kutta for time integration and a novel implicit-explicit splitting scheme for Faraday's law to relax timestep constraints.
AI.result: The model successfully reproduces cold plasma wave branches, ion Bernstein waves, compressional and shear Alfvén waves, and ion acoustic waves in slab geometry validation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quasi-neutral hybrid model with implicit-explicit splitting effectively relaxes the timestep restriction while accurately capturing a wide range of low-frequency plasma waves.

title: Spectral distribution of Jacobi weighted histopolation matrices via GLT theory
summary: In this paper we study a weighted histopolation problem on $[-1,1]$ associated with Jacobi weights. In the first part of the present work we prove results in approximation theory, while in the second we analyze the resulting matrices from an asymptotic linear algebra perspective. More in detail, in the first part, given weighted cell averages, we construct a reconstruction operator based on weighted primitives of Jacobi polynomials and investigate the resulting discretization matrices. At any fixed discretization level, we derive an exact factorization of the histopolation matrix through a backward-difference operator and a sampling operator of Jacobi weighted primitives. Combining a sharp integration by parts identity with the three-term recurrence of Jacobi polynomials, we further show that the primitive sampling operator admits an explicit decomposition involving a tridiagonal coupling matrix in the Jacobi spectral index. This yields a tridiagonal factor representation of the histopolation matrix. In the second part, under standard mesh-regularity assumptions, we show that all the various induced matrix sequences belong to the Generalized Locally Toeplitz (GLT) class, by describing in detail the related GLT symbols. As a consequence, we provide the corresponding spectral distributions and discuss their implications for numerical stability when solving the associated linear systems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Jacobi-weighted histopolation on [-1,1], deriving exact factorizations and tridiagonal representations of histopolation matrices, then analyzing their asymptotic spectral properties via GLT theory.
AI.motivation: To bridge weighted histopolation (reconstruction from cell averages) with polynomial approximation theory and asymptotic linear algebra, particularly to understand matrix structures and spectral behavior for numerical stability.
AI.method: Uses weighted primitives of Jacobi polynomials, backward-difference operators, integration by parts, and three-term recurrences to factor histopolation matrices. Applies Generalized Locally Toeplitz (GLT) theory for asymptotic spectral analysis.
AI.result: Exact factorization of histopolation matrix through backward-difference and primitive sampling operators; explicit tridiagonal factor representation; classification of induced matrix sequences under the GLT class with detailed symbols.
AI.conclusion: The spectral distributions derived from GLT analysis provide insights into numerical stability when solving associated linear systems from weighted histopolation.

title: Strichartz estimates for Schrödinger equations with nonlinear boundary interactions
summary:
We study a Schrödinger equation in the upper half-space with a nonlinear Neumann boundary interaction driven by the Bessel operator $\Ba$, $a>-1$. The problem arises naturally as an extension formulation for a nonlocal NLS with memory and can also be interpreted as a Schrödinger evolution with a nonlinear singular source concentrated on a codimension-one interface.
  We first develop a complete linear theory for the associated inhomogeneous problem with nonhomogeneous Neumann data. A central ingredient is a new Duhamel representation formula that separates bulk and boundary dynamics and identifies the precise role of the boundary propagator. Using this formula, we establish sharp Strichartz estimates adapted to the geometry of the half-space and the singular structure induced by the Bessel operator. The analysis reveals a basic dichotomy between the regimes $a\ge 0$ and $-1<a<0$: in the former, bulk and boundary exhibit a unified dispersive behavior, whereas in the latter the dispersive structure becomes anomalous, requiring weighted estimates and distinct functional frameworks for the bulk and boundary contributions.
  As an application of the linear theory, we prove well-posedness results for the nonlinear problem in the $L_a^2$-mass critical and subcritical regimes. For $a\ge 0$, we obtain global well-posedness for sufficiently small critical data, together with local and global results in the subcritical case. In the anomalous range $-1<a<0$, we establish existence and uniqueness on arbitrary finite time intervals for sufficiently small critical data, as well as local well-posedness in the subcritical regime. The results provide a unified dispersive framework for Schrödinger equations with nonlinear boundary interactions and singular extension structures associated with nonlocal-in-time dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a complete linear theory for a Schrödinger equation in the upper half-space with nonlinear Neumann boundary interaction driven by the Bessel operator, revealing a dichotomy between $a\ge 0$ (unified dispersive behavior) and $-1<a<0$ (anomalous regime requiring weighted estimates). Well-posedness results are proved for the nonlinear problem in both critical and subcritical regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand Schrödinger equations with nonlinear Neumann boundary interactions, which arise as extension formulations for nonlocal NLS with memory and as Schrödinger evolutions with nonlinear singular sources on codimension-one interfaces.
AI.method: Develops a Duhamel representation formula separating bulk and boundary dynamics; establishes sharp Strichartz estimates adapted to the half-space geometry and Bessel operator singular structure; analyzes the dichotomy between $a\ge 0$ and $-1<a<0$ regimes.
AI.result: For $a\ge 0$: global well-posedness for small critical data, local/global results for subcritical case. For $-1<a<0$: existence/uniqueness on finite time intervals for small critical data, local well-posedness for subcritical regime.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified dispersive framework for Schrödinger equations with nonlinear boundary interactions and singular extension structures associated with nonlocal-in-time dynamics.

title: Adjoint inverse design of microresonator frequency combs
summary: Microresonator frequency combs are key components for integrating optical devices into photonic circuits. They provide high stability, coherence, and low noise, even without external stabilization. Yet microcomb design remains largely heuristic: waveguide and resonator parameters are typically swept manually or semi-empirically, and the resulting spectra are evaluated only afterwards. This forward-design workflow is computationally costly, relies heavily on designer intuition, and does not generally identify optimal solutions. Here, we present an adjoint-based inverse-design framework for microresonator frequency combs that directly optimizes the comb spectrum with respect to pre-defined objectives. We demonstrate the power and flexibility of this approach by addressing three challenging problems: designing spectrally flat combs, synthesizing arbitrarily shaped comb spectra, and enforcing several performance metrics simultaneously through multi-objective optimization. Our results show that inverse design offers a systematic and efficient route to compact on-chip light sources with properties tailored to diverse applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an adjoint-based inverse-design framework for microresonator frequency combs that directly optimizes comb spectra toward predefined objectives, demonstrating its effectiveness on three challenging problems.
AI.motivation: Current microcomb design is largely heuristic—waveguide and resonator parameters are manually swept or semi-empirically tuned, and spectra are evaluated only after fabrication. This forward-design workflow is computationally costly, relies heavily on designer intuition, and fails to systematically identify optimal solutions.
AI.method: The authors propose an adjoint-based inverse-design framework that directly optimizes the comb spectrum against predefined objectives, enabling systematic optimization of microresonator frequency comb parameters.
AI.result: The framework successfully addresses three challenging problems: (1) designing spectrally flat combs, (2) synthesizing arbitrarily shaped comb spectra, and (3) enforcing multiple performance metrics simultaneously through multi-objective optimization.
AI.conclusion: Inverse design offers a systematic and efficient route to compact on-chip light sources with properties tailored to diverse applications, outperforming traditional heuristic approaches.

title: On the treatment of topology changes on 3D polyhedral moving meshes via 4D space-time hole-like elements in direct ALE ADER-DG methods
summary: This work investigates a novel approach for the high order evolution of hyperbolic PDEs using ADER discontinuous Galerkin schemes within a direct Arbitrary-Lagrangian-Eulerian (ALE) framework on 3D moving polyhedral meshes with topology changes. Our direct ALE method is based on the PDE integration over 4D (3D+time) space-time control volumes connecting the elements of two subsequent tessellations, so to simultaneously evolve the solution both in time and between the two different meshes in an effective and high order manner. In this way, we also avoid any complex and expensive projection-reconstruction techniques and any mesh intersection operation typical of indirect ALE schemes. The crucial step consists in the strategy for building space-time control volumes that also connect elements with different shapes and neighborhoods due to a change in topology. In fact, simply linking existing elements by collapsing or expanding their edges would leave a "hole" in the space-time domain. To fill it, we introduce additional degenerate elements that we call hole-like elements. These are 4D objects with zero 3D volume at both the beginning and end of the timestep, but which possess a strictly non-zero 4D space-time volume. Given the uniqueness of this space-time approach in 3D+time and the necessity of characterizing the geometry of such elements, the main objective of this paper is the formal geometrical and numerical description of the method as well as the presentation of new and intuitive visualization strategies. In particular, we provide a detailed characterization of the hole-like elements arising in correspondence to 2-3, 3-2, and 4-4 flips on the underlying Delaunay tetrahedralization. Finally, we numerically show that the method is fully conservative, satisfies the GCL and maintains the correct order of convergence even in the presence of frequent topology changes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel ADER-DG scheme on 3D moving polyhedral meshes with topology changes using a direct ALE approach based on 4D space-time control volumes. It introduces "hole-like elements" to handle topology changes and provides comprehensive geometrical characterization and numerical validation showing conservation, GCL satisfaction, and high-order accuracy.
AI.motivation: To enable high-order accurate evolution of hyperbolic PDEs on 3D moving meshes with topology changes, avoiding expensive projection-reconstruction and mesh intersection operations typical of indirect ALE methods.
AI.method: A direct ALE-ADER-DG scheme using 4D (3D+time) space-time control volumes connecting successive tessellations. Hole-like degenerate elements (with zero 3D volume but non-zero 4D volume) are introduced to fill gaps from topology changes such as 2-3, 3-2, and 4-4 flips on Delaunay tetrahedralizations.
AI.result: The method is fully conservative, satisfies the Geometric Conservation Law (GCL), and maintains correct high-order convergence even with frequent topology changes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed direct ALE framework with hole-like elements effectively handles topology changes on 3D moving polyhedral meshes while preserving high-order accuracy, conservation, and GCL compliance, eliminating the need for complex projection-reconstruction techniques.

title: Global uniform regularity and vanishing vertical viscosity limit for the compressible Navier--Stokes equations in the half-space
summary: In geophysical flows such as large-scale ocean dynamics, the vertical viscosity is often much smaller than the horizontal viscosity. This anisotropy makes it natural to ask whether solutions of the full anisotropic compressible Navier--Stokes equations converge, as the vertical viscosity coefficient $\varepsilon \to 0$, to solutions of a horizontally dissipative limit system, and whether this limit can be justified globally in time. Prior work has answered this question locally in time or in the incompressible setting. We resolve this problem for the three-dimensional compressible Navier--Stokes equations in the upper half-space with the Navier slip boundary condition. This paper establishes two main results for small perturbations of a constant equilibrium state. First, we prove the existence of a unique global-in-time solution whose conormal Sobolev norm remains uniformly bounded for all $t \ge 0$ and all $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$. Second, we justify the global vanishing vertical viscosity limit. More precisely, we show that the solutions converge to a global solution of the horizontally dissipative compressible Navier--Stokes system.
