This provides the first rigorous justification of the anisotropic viscosity limit for compressible flows on an infinite time interval.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global-in-time existence and vanishing vertical viscosity limit for the 3D compressible Navier-Stokes equations with Navier slip boundary conditions on the upper half-space, for small perturbations of a constant equilibrium state.
AI.motivation: In geophysical flows, vertical viscosity is often much smaller than horizontal viscosity. The authors aim to determine whether solutions of the anisotropic compressible Navier-Stokes equations converge to a horizontally dissipative limit system as vertical viscosity goes to zero, and whether this limit can be justified globally in time — a question previously answered only locally in time or for incompressible flows.
AI.method: The authors study the 3D compressible Navier-Stokes equations in the upper half-space with Navier slip boundary conditions, focusing on small perturbations around a constant equilibrium. They employ conormal Sobolev norms to obtain uniform bounds for all ε∈(0,1) and all t≥0, then use these bounds to justify the vanishing vertical viscosity limit.
AI.result: Two main results: (1) Existence of a unique global-in-time solution with uniformly bounded conormal Sobolev norm for all t≥0 and ε∈(0,1). (2) The solutions converge globally to a solution of the horizontally dissipative compressible Navier-Stokes system as ε→0, providing the first rigorous justification of the anisotropic viscosity limit for compressible flows on an infinite time interval.
AI.conclusion: The paper resolves a long-standing open problem by proving the global vanishing vertical viscosity limit for compressible Navier-Stokes equations, confirming that in the anisotropic limit, the dynamics are governed by a horizontally dissipative system even over infinite time horizons.

title: The physics of AI weather models
summary: Could it be that AI weather models are solving physical equations, although they may not be the equations used by conventional NWP models? We compute correlations of forecast skill and Centered Kernel Alignment, providing evidence that different AI weather models represent the atmosphere in similar ways, despite differences in architecture and capacity. We argue that the architecture and training of the AI models constrains the form of the physical laws that they might simulate. In particular, we propose that the models implement a particle description of the atmosphere, where the latent variables at each mesh point correspond to the position of a particle in the high dimensional latent space. We hypothesize that the movement of the particles follows a gradient flow in the latent space towards a minimum of a learned free energy functional. Analysis of the GraphCast and Aurora models show that they make changes on large spatial scales in the early processor layers and move to smaller scale with increasing layer depth, consistent with the gradient flow hypothesis.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how AI weather models may implicitly solve physical equations by analyzing their internal representations. The authors find that different AI weather models represent the atmosphere similarly, despite architectural differences, and propose that these models implement a particle-based description with gradient flow dynamics in latent space toward minimizing a learned free energy functional.
AI.motivation: To understand whether AI weather models are learning and solving physical equations (potentially different from conventional NWP equations), and to uncover what underlying physical principles these black-box models might be implicitly implementing.
AI.method: The authors compute correlations of forecast skill and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to compare representations across different AI weather models. They analyze GraphCast and Aurora models to examine how changes occur across spatial scales at different processor layers, testing the gradient flow hypothesis.
AI.result: Different AI weather models represent the atmosphere in similar ways despite differences in architecture and capacity. Analysis shows that models make changes on large spatial scales in early layers and transition to smaller scales with increasing depth, consistent with a gradient flow mechanism.
AI.conclusion: AI weather models likely implement a particle description of the atmosphere where latent variables correspond to particle positions, moving via gradient flow toward a minimum of a learned free energy functional, suggesting these models implicitly solve physical equations.

title: Accelerating an ensemble of variational data assimilations with randomized preconditioning
summary: Ensembles of variational data assimilations (EDA) require solving systems of linear equations with iterative methods. The solution process can be accelerated using a limited memory preconditioner constructed with approximations of the leading eigenpairs of the Hessian matrix. Randomized methods for low-rank matrix approximations provide a feasible approach for computing these eigenpairs. These methods use a random sketching matrix to obtain a low-rank representation of the Hessian matrix, which is then used for computing the eigendecomposition. The sketching matrix highly influences the quality of the approximation. In this paper, we show how the structure of the EDA can be exploited to construct a suitable sketching matrix, i.e., using the differences of the right-hand sides of the linear systems of equations. Idealised numerical experiments with the Lorenz-96 model show that the resulting preconditioner is able to accelerate the EDA solution process for all ensemble members, even if constructed from the control member only.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using ensemble differences from EDA as a sketching matrix for randomized low-rank Hessian approximations, constructing efficient preconditioners that accelerate linear system solving for all ensemble members.
AI.motivation: EDA requires solving many linear systems iteratively, which is computationally expensive; existing randomized preconditioners rely on random sketching matrices whose quality is variable and suboptimal for EDA's structure.
AI.method: Exploiting EDA's structure by using differences of right-hand sides (ensemble perturbations) as the sketching matrix to build a limited memory preconditioner from approximate Hessian eigenpairs.
AI.result: On Lorenz-96 model experiments, the preconditioner accelerates convergence for all ensemble members, even when constructed solely from the control member's information.
AI.conclusion: Using EDA-inherent ensemble differences as sketching matrices yields effective, computationally efficient preconditioners for variational data assimilation.

title: Optimal semiconcavity with fractional modulus for Hamilton-Jacobi equations with Neumann boundary conditions
summary: Here, we study the generalized semiconcavity property of viscosity solutions of the Neumann boundary value problem for Hamilton-Jacobi equations. In particular, we establish the global semiconcavity with a fractional modulus by investigating a regularity property of solutions to the Skorokhod problem, and show the optimality of the fractional exponent.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes global semiconcavity with a fractional modulus for viscosity solutions of Neumann boundary value problems for Hamilton-Jacobi equations, and proves the optimality of the fractional exponent via Skorokhod problem regularity.
AI.motivation: To study the generalized semiconcavity property of viscosity solutions for Hamilton-Jacobi equations with Neumann boundary conditions, and determine optimal regularity exponents.
AI.method: Investigates the regularity property of solutions to the Skorokhod problem to establish global semiconcavity with a fractional modulus.
AI.result: Established global semiconcavity with a fractional modulus and showed the optimality of the fractional exponent.
AI.conclusion: The global semiconcavity property is obtained with a fractional modulus, and the fractional exponent is optimal.

title: Vapor-Cell-Induced Uncertainty in Rydberg Atom Measurements via the Electric-Field Volume-Integral-Equation Method
summary: Electromagnetic scattering effects of a vapor cell on electric-field measurements using Rydberg atom-based sensors are analyzed with the aid of the volume integral equation method. In a manner similar to measurement, this computational approach determines the electric field over grid points within the vapor cell. Its relatively high computational efficiency makes it suitable for use in optimization routines and statistical uncertainty studies. We apply this method to compare uncertainty contributions arising due to the presence of the vapor cell, such as uncertainty in the glass relative permittivity or standing wave formation inside the cell, to those arising from the atomic spectroscopic measurement, such as uncertainty in the atomic dipole moment. For vapor cell dimensions less than half a wavelength, the dominant uncertainty source arises from uncertainty in the glass relative permittivity, resulting in a total uncertainty of $\sim$3.5\% -- comparable to the best uncertainties obtained with traditional field generation methods at national metrology institutes. Precise permittivity measurements have the potential to further reduce measurement uncertainty to $<1$\%.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a volume integral equation method to analyze how electromagnetic scattering from a vapor cell affects electric field measurements in Rydberg atom-based sensors. It compares uncertainty sources and finds that for cells smaller than half a wavelength, the glass permittivity uncertainty dominates (~3.5% total uncertainty), with potential to reduce below 1% with precise permittivity measurements.
AI.motivation: To accurately quantify and compare uncertainty contributions from vapor cell scattering effects versus atomic spectroscopic measurements in Rydberg atom-based electric field sensors.
AI.method: Volume integral equation method for computing electric fields over grid points inside vapor cells, enabling efficient optimization and statistical uncertainty analysis.
AI.result: For cells under half-wavelength, dominant uncertainty is from glass relative permittivity (~3.5% total uncertainty); atomic dipole moment uncertainty is less significant.
AI.conclusion: The computational method is efficient and accurate for characterizing vapor cell effects; with precise permittivity measurements, total uncertainty can be reduced below 1%.

title: Mixed-Precision in adaptive Runge-Kutta method for large ODE systems
comment: Preprint of an article submitted to SIAM Journal on Scientific Computing (under revision)
summary: Mixed-precision methods combine low and high precision arithmetics to exploit low precision computational speed and high precision accuracy. Large ODE systems that contain many heterogeneous interactions lead to a high computational cost that could be tackled with mixed-precision solvers. We tested mixedprecision versions of the Bogacki-Shampine 3(2) Runge-Kutta pair over three benchmark systems: coupled linear oscillators, the Kuramoto model and a circadian clock model. Our study is performed in a way that can be adapted to any finite-precision format, software architecture and numerical scheme. We found that mixed-precision solvers can preserve most of the high-precision solver accuracy under a wide range of solver tolerances. Moreover, mixed-precision solver accuracy improves with system size, reaching levels equivalent to high-precision solvers in small system size. We also observed that mixed-precision arithmetic does not impact the number of evaluation in a way that balances the benefit of fast operations in low precision. Taken together, these results show that mixed-precision methods can offer significant computational speed-up at little or no loss of accuracy in large coupled ODE systems.

AI.tldr: This paper tests mixed-precision solvers (combining low and high precision arithmetic) on large coupled ODE systems and finds they preserve high accuracy while offering significant computational speed-up, especially for large systems.
AI.motivation: Large ODE systems with heterogeneous interactions incur high computational costs; mixed-precision methods could exploit low precision speed while maintaining high precision accuracy.
AI.method: Tested mixed-precision versions of the Bogacki-Shampine 3(2) Runge-Kutta pair on three benchmark systems (coupled linear oscillators, Kuramoto model, circadian clock model) in a format-adaptive manner.
AI.result: Mixed-precision solvers preserve high-precision accuracy across wide tolerances; accuracy improves with system size, matching high-precision solvers for small systems; precision does not negatively impact evaluation count.
AI.conclusion: Mixed-precision methods offer significant computational speed-up with little or no accuracy loss in large coupled ODE systems.

title: Nonlinear Transmission Eigenvalue Problems with Nonhomogeneous Operators of Different p-Growth
summary: Let $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^N$, $N \ge 2$, be a bounded domain with Lipschitz boundary, divided by a Lipschitz hypersurface $Σ$ into two open, disjoint Lipschitz subdomains $Ω_1$ and $Ω_2$. We study a nonlinear transmission eigenvalue problem driven by nonhomogeneous operators with $p_i$- growth in each subdomain $Ω_i$, $i=1,2$, and subject to continuity and flux transmission conditions across the interface $Σ$. The real parameter $λ$ appears both in the equations and in the nonlinear boundary conditions. Using variational methods, we prove the existence of an unbounded sequence of eigenvalues. Under additional assumptions, we establish that the set of eigenvalues coincides with the entire interval $(0,\infty)$. As a particular case, we obtain the corresponding eigenvalue results for the associated single-domain problem.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a nonlinear transmission eigenvalue problem with p_i-growth operators on a domain split by an interface, proving existence of an unbounded eigenvalue sequence via variational methods, and showing under extra assumptions the eigenvalue set equals (0,∞).
AI.motivation: To analyze eigenvalue problems in composite materials or multi-domain settings where different material properties (p_i-growth) exist in each subdomain, connected by transmission conditions across the interface.
AI.method: Variational methods are used to analyze the nonlinear transmission eigenvalue problem with nonhomogeneous p_i-growth operators, subject to continuity and flux transmission conditions.
AI.result: An unbounded sequence of eigenvalues exists. Under additional assumptions, the eigenvalue set covers the entire positive real line (0,∞).
AI.conclusion: The results generalize eigenvalue theory to multi-domain transmission problems with distinct growth exponents, and recover single-domain eigenvalue results as a special case.

title: Expand More, Shrink Less: Shaping Effective-Rank Dynamics for Dense Scaling in Recommendation
comment: Accepted at the 32st ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (Research Track), KDD 2026 February Cycle
summary: Scaling recommendation models is a central challenge in recommender systems. Recently, RankMixer has emerged as an effective solution, operating on a unified token representation and alternating between token mixing and per-token feedforward networks (P-FFNs) to achieve scalable performance. However, RankMixer suffers from \textit{embedding collapse}, where learned representations have low effective rank, limiting expressivity and underutilizing the expanded representation space. Through empirical analysis and theoretical insights, we identify rigid token mixing and P-FFN modules as the primary causes of this phenomenon, jointly inducing a \textbf{damped oscillatory trajectory} in effective-rank evolution across layers. To address it, we propose RankElastor, a novel architecture that produces spectrum-robust representations with provable collapse mitigation. RankElastor introduces two components: (i) \textbf{parameterized full mixing}, which enables expressive token mixing with improved spectral robustness; and (ii) \textbf{GLU-improved P-FFNs}, which stabilize representation spectra through GLU-style FFN modules. Extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets demonstrate that RankElastor consistently improves recommendation performance, mitigates embedding collapse, and exhibits robust scaling behavior. Code is available at this GitHub repository: https://github.com/vasile-paskardlgm/RankElastor

AI.tldr: RankElastor addresses embedding collapse in RankMixer by introducing parameterized full mixing and GLU-improved P-FFNs, achieving more expressive, spectrum-robust representations with better scaling performance.
AI.motivation: Scaling recommendation models is challenging. RankMixer suffers from embedding collapse—low effective rank representations that limit expressivity and underutilize the expanded representation space—caused by rigid token mixing and P-FFN modules.
AI.method: RankElastor introduces two components: (i) parameterized full mixing for expressive token mixing with improved spectral robustness; and (ii) GLU-improved P-FFNs to stabilize representation spectra via GLU-style FFN modules.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets show RankElastor consistently improves recommendation performance, mitigates embedding collapse, and exhibits robust scaling behavior.
AI.conclusion: RankElastor effectively mitigates embedding collapse in scaling recommendation models, providing provable collapse mitigation and robust scaling behavior.

title: Local well-posedness for a moving rigid region in Surface Quasi-Geostrophic equations
summary: We introduce and analyze a class of Surface Quasi-Geostrophic (SQG) equations in the presence of moving rigid obstacles. The model is motivated both by vortex-wave type asymptotics for singular structures in active scalar equations and by geophysical phenomena exhibiting rigid-like coherent regions, such as cyclone eyes or long-lived atmospheric dust clouds. We consider the critical SQG equation in a time-dependent exterior domain generated by a prescribed rigid motion and reconstruct the velocity through a nonlocal elliptic formulation adapted to impermeability constraints. The active scalar is assumed to remain constant inside the rigid region and in a neighborhood of its boundary, yielding a plateau structure compatible with the transport dynamics. For a single moving obstacle, we establish local well-posedness of classical solutions in Sobolev spaces $H^k$, $k\geq 4$ together with uniqueness, local stability, and a blow-up criterion. The analysis relies on a reformulation in adapted coordinates reducing the problem to a fixed domain, combined with integral representations for the fractional elliptic operator, regularization procedures, and a nonlinear fixed-point argument. A central difficulty comes from the critical singularity of the SQG Biot-Savart kernel in the case $s=\frac{1}{2}$, for which the velocity reconstruction near the moving boundary requires commutator estimates. We further prove propagation of the plateau property and derive a priori estimates controlling both the support of the scalar gradient and the Sobolev norm of the solution. This work provides, to our knowledge, the first well-posedness theory for SQG equations with moving rigid obstacles and constitutes a first step toward the rigorous derivation of point-vortex type dynamics from shrinking rigid bodies in SQG flows.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the first well-posedness theory for critical Surface Quasi-Geostrophic (SQG) equations with moving rigid obstacles, proving local existence, uniqueness, stability, and blow-up criteria for classical solutions in Sobolev spaces H^k (k≥4), using adapted coordinates, integral representations, and commutator estimates to handle the critical singularity.
AI.motivation: The work is motivated by vortex-wave asymptotics for singular structures in active scalar equations and geophysical phenomena like cyclone eyes or long-lived atmospheric dust clouds, aiming to provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for SQG equations with moving rigid boundaries.
AI.method: The analysis uses a reformulation in adapted coordinates to reduce the time-dependent exterior domain to a fixed domain, combined with integral representations for the fractional elliptic operator, regularization procedures, commutator estimates for the critical SQG Biot-Savart kernel, and a nonlinear fixed-point argument.
AI.result: Local well-posedness of classical solutions in Sobolev spaces H^k (k≥4) is established for a single moving obstacle, along with uniqueness, local stability, a blow-up criterion, propagation of the plateau property, and a priori estimates controlling both the scalar gradient support and Sobolev norms.
AI.conclusion: This is the first well-posedness theory for SQG equations with moving rigid obstacles, representing a foundational step toward rigorous derivation of point-vortex type dynamics from shrinking rigid bodies in SQG flows.

title: Coupling-Robust Accuracy in Multiphysics Physics Informed Neural Networks via Kronecker-Preconditioned Optimization
comment: 20 pages, 10 figures. Extended version of AI4Physics Workshop submission (ICML 2026)
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for coupled multiphysics systems suffer systematic accuracy degradation as inter-equation coupling strengthens. We provide a theoretical explanation for this phenomenon through neural tangent kernel (NTK) analysis: for linearly coupled systems, we prove that the standard NTK's spectral radius grows as $Ω(γ^2)$ with coupling strength $γ$, shrinking the stable learning rate, while block-diagonal Gauss--Newton (GN) preconditioning yields a preconditioned NTK $K_P = J H^{+} J^\top$ (where $H$ is the block-diagonal GN Hessian) whose spectral radius is bounded by $S$ ($S$ = number of networks), independent of $γ$. We verify the $Ω(γ^2)$ growth numerically across symmetric, asymmetric, and nonlinear coupled PDE systems, and confirm $λ_{\max}(K_P) = S$ with equality in all cases. Combining the Kronecker-preconditioned optimizer SOAP with inverse-gradient-norm loss balancing (SOAP+GN) yields coupling-robust accuracy: across 234 experiments spanning three 1D systems of increasing nonlinearity and a 2D electroosmotic flow benchmark, SOAP+GN maintains final-epoch $L_2$ degradation $\leq 1.1\times$ (ratio of strong- to weak-coupling error) even as coupling parameters vary over one to two orders of magnitude, compared with $> 10^2\times$ for Adam+GN. SOAP+GN further scales to a 2D, 6-PDE electroosmotic flow system at EDL-resolved conditions -- a regime that all prior PINN electrokinetics studies have avoided through simplified physics -- where Adam+GN fails entirely ($L_2 > 0.9$).

AI.tldr: PINNs degrade under strong coupling in multiphysics systems. The authors prove via NTK analysis that coupling strength γ enlarges spectral radius, shrinking learnable rates. They propose SOAP+GN, a Kronecker-preconditioned optimizer with inverse-gradient-norm loss balancing, which achieves coupling-robust accuracy (≤1.1× degradation) across 234 experiments, and scales to a 2D 6-PDE electroosmotic flow system where Adam+GN fails.
AI.motivation: To understand and fix the systematic accuracy degradation of PINNs in coupled multiphysics systems as inter-equation coupling strengthens.
AI.method: Theoretical NTK analysis proving spectral radius grows as Ω(γ²); block-diagonal Gauss-Newton preconditioning yields bounded spectral radius (S, independent of γ); SOAP+GN combines Kronecker-preconditioned optimizer SOAP with inverse-gradient-norm loss balancing.
AI.result: SOAP+GN maintains final-epoch L₂ degradation ≤1.1× across 234 experiments (1D and 2D systems), compared to >10²× for Adam+GN. Scales to 2D 6-PDE electroosmotic flow at EDL-resolved conditions where Adam+GN fails (L₂ > 0.9).
AI.conclusion: Coupling-induced spectral growth in NTK causes accuracy degradation; SOAP+GN preconditioning eliminates coupling-dependent spectral radius, enabling robust multiphysics PINN training.

title: Solution of a Simple Case of the Navier-Stokes Equations via Employing the Lambert W Function
comment: This paper was presented at the Scientific Student Conference of Eötvös Loránd University on Nov. 26, 2003 with the same title. (Contains 6 pages with 0 figures.)
summary: The purpose of this paper is to introduce a curious function of two variables, expressable via the employment of the Lambert W Funtion, which can be generalized to satisfy Euler's Equation of Inviscid Motion over a specific domain, with pressure independent of space variables.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a two-variable function expressed via the Lambert W Function, which generalizes to satisfy Euler's Equation of Inviscid Motion under specific conditions where pressure is spatially independent.
AI.motivation: To introduce and explore a curious two-variable function using the Lambert W Function that satisfies Euler's inviscid flow equation under special conditions.
AI.method: The authors define a function of two variables expressed via the Lambert W Function and analyze its properties to show it satisfies Euler's Equation of Inviscid Motion in a domain where pressure is space-independent.
AI.result: The proposed function can be generalized to satisfy Euler's Equation of Inviscid Motion over a specific domain with pressure independent of spatial variables.
AI.conclusion: A novel function expressed via the Lambert W Function can be used to satisfy Euler's inviscid motion equations under spatially independent pressure conditions.

title: Stochastic Generalized Sampling
summary: Reconstructing an infinite-dimensional signal from a finite set of measurements is a fundamental problem in approximation theory and signal processing. While the generalized sampling (GS) framework provides a robust methodology for recovering elements in arbitrary separable Hilbert spaces, deterministic approaches suffer from severe basis-dependent dimensionality constraints, often requiring a quadratic sample complexity $m \gtrsim n^2$ to avoid numerical instability. In this paper, we introduce a fully stochastic framework for GS that natively overcomes these deterministic barriers. By drawing measurements according to an optimal leverage-score probability distribution, we prove that stable recovery is guaranteed with high probability at a near-linear sample complexity of $m \gtrsim n\log n$. Crucially, this optimal rate is universal-independent of the specific choice of measurement and reconstruction bases-and holds even when the sensing system is a highly redundant frame. To establish these guarantees, we derive a novel matrix Bernstein inequality for random rectangular operators, allowing us to rigorously control the aliasing error governed by the empirical cross-term. Finally, we demonstrate the practical efficacy of our approach on the classical problem of recovering analytic functions from continuous Fourier measurements via Legendre polynomials, where our randomized method achieve near-exponential convergence rates.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a stochastic generalized sampling framework that overcomes deterministic dimensionality barriers, achieving near-linear sample complexity (m ≳ n log n) via optimal leverage-score sampling, with universality across bases and demonstrated efficacy on Fourier-Legendre recovery.
AI.motivation: Deterministic generalized sampling suffers from severe basis-dependent dimensionality constraints, often requiring quadratic sample complexity m ≳ n² to avoid numerical instability, which limits practical applicability.
AI.method: A fully stochastic generalized sampling framework is introduced, where measurements are drawn according to an optimal leverage-score probability distribution. A novel matrix Bernstein inequality for random rectangular operators is derived to control the aliasing error.
AI.result: Stable recovery is guaranteed with high probability at near-linear sample complexity m ≳ n log n, which is universal—independent of the choice of measurement and reconstruction bases—and holds even for highly redundant frames.
AI.conclusion: The randomized method achieves near-exponential convergence rates in practice, as demonstrated on recovering analytic functions from continuous Fourier measurements via Legendre polynomials.

title: Approximate controllability in small times of bilinear Schr{ö}dinger equations with magnetic drift
summary: We study the small-time approximate controllability of bilinear Schr{ö}dinger equations, where the drift is a magnetic Schr{ö}dinger operator and the control is an electric potential. We prove this property in two circumstances: (i) in $\mathbb{R}^d$, with a quadratic and an additional generic bounded electric potential in the control, and with a uniform magnetic field in the drift; (ii) in $\mathbb{R}^d$ or $\mathbb{T}^d$, with control electric potentials supported on a finite number of Hermite or Fourier eigenfunctions, and with any differentiable magnetic potential in the drift.

AI.tldr: The paper proves small-time approximate controllability for bilinear Schrödinger equations with magnetic drift and electric potential control, under two specific conditions involving quadratic potentials, bounded potentials, or eigenfunction-supported controls.
AI.motivation: To establish small-time approximate controllability of bilinear Schrödinger equations with magnetic fields, which is a fundamental control-theoretic property for quantum systems under realistic physical constraints.
AI.method: The authors analyze two scenarios: (i) in ℝ^d with quadratic + generic bounded electric control potential and uniform magnetic drift; (ii) in ℝ^d or 𝕋^d with control potentials supported on finitely many Hermite/Fourier eigenfunctions and differentiable magnetic drift potentials.
AI.result: Small-time approximate controllability is proven under both specified conditions, demonstrating controllability with physically relevant potential shapes and limited control resources.
AI.conclusion: Bilinear Schrödinger equations with magnetic drift can be approximately controlled in arbitrarily small time, even when controls are restricted to specific potential forms or finite-dimensional eigenfunction subspaces.

title: RA-DCA: A Randomized Active-Set DCA for Directional Stationarity in Max-Structured DC Programs
summary: We study nonsmooth difference-of-convex programs whose subtracted convex term is a finite maximum of smooth convex functions. In this setting, standard DCA iterations may converge to critical points that are not directionally stationary, whereas exact active-vertex screening can be expensive when active sets are large or combinatorial. We propose RA-DCA, a vertex-first randomized active-set DCA that projects active gradients onto sampled directions, checks a sampled vertex residual, and uses a small linear program only as a low-residual convex-combination fallback. The method preserves the descent structure of DCA and reduces the randomized screening layer to matrix multiplications. Under the stated regularity, numerical active-set consistency, and random-embedding assumptions, every accumulation point generated by the safeguarded method is directionally stationary with probability one. MATLAB experiments first test the theorem on degenerate max-affine, max-quadratic, and sparse support-function models, where the safeguard avoids nonstationary critical points and closely tracks a full active-vertex scan. Block top-k tests then show that the same screening idea remains useful when exact aggregate enumeration is combinatorial. Trimmed-regression, complementarity, and QUBO diagnostics separate cases where active-set selection helps from cases dominated by multistart search, the DC split, or other problem-specific features.

AI.tldr: RA-DCA is a randomized active-set DCA method for nonsmooth difference-of-convex programs that ensures directional stationarity with probability one, avoiding nonstationary critical points common in standard DCA, while reducing screening to efficient matrix operations.
AI.motivation: Standard DCA iterations may converge to critical points that are not directionally stationary, and exact active-vertex screening is expensive when active sets are large or combinatorial.
AI.method: RA-DCA (vertex-first randomized active-set DCA) projects active gradients onto sampled directions, checks a sampled vertex residual, and uses a small linear program only as a low-residual convex-combination fallback, reducing the screening layer to matrix multiplications.
AI.result: Under regularity, active-set consistency, and random-embedding assumptions, every accumulation point is directionally stationary with probability one. MATLAB experiments validate the method on degenerate max-affine, max-quadratic, and sparse support-function models, and block top-k tests show the screening idea remains useful when exact enumeration is combinatorial.
AI.conclusion: The safeguarded RA-DCA avoids nonstationary critical points, closely tracks full active-vertex scans, and is useful across trimmed-regression, complementarity, and QUBO diagnostics, though its effectiveness depends on problem-specific features.

title: A Priori Regularity Estimates for Ratio of Solutions to Elliptic Equations with a Product Structure of Two-Dimensional Nodal Sets
summary: In this paper, we establish optimal a priori $C^{1,α}$ regularity estimates for the ratio $w = v/u$ of two solutions to the same elliptic equation $-\operatorname{div}(A \nabla u )=0$ with Lipschitz coefficients $A$, under the assumption that their nodal sets satisfy $Z(u) \subseteq Z(v)$. We specifically address the case where the zero set $Z(u)$ exhibits a product structure of $2$-dimensional nodal sets, namely $Z(u)=Z(u_1)\times \cdots \times Z(u_{m})$, where the $u_i$ are $2$-dimensional functions. This result extends the regularity estimates previously proved in dimension $2$ by [Logunov and Malinnikova, 2016] and by [Terracini, Tortone, and Vita, 2026].

AI.tldr: Establishes optimal C^{1,α} regularity for ratios of solutions to elliptic equations with Lipschitz coefficients, extending 2D results to higher dimensions where nodal sets have a product structure.
AI.motivation: To generalize prior 2D regularity estimates for the ratio of two elliptic solutions (Logunov-Malinnikova 2016, Terracini-Tortone-Vita 2026) to higher-dimensional settings where the nodal set has a product structure.
AI.method: Assumes elliptic equation with Lipschitz coefficients A, solutions u and v with Z(u) ⊆ Z(v), and nodal set Z(u) having a product structure of 2D nodal sets. Proves optimal a priori C^{1,α} regularity for w = v/u.
AI.result: Optimal C^{1,α} regularity estimates for the ratio w = v/u are established under the product-structure assumption on Z(u), extending prior 2D results to higher dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully generalizes known 2D regularity results to higher dimensions under specific nodal set structure conditions, providing optimal estimates.

title: Training-Free Looped Transformers
summary: We introduce training-free looped transformers, in which a lightweight inference-time wrapper loops a contiguous mid-stack block of layers of a frozen checkpoint without additional fine-tuning, continued training, or architectural changes. Unlike prior looped transformer methods that train with the looped structure end-to-end, we retrofit recurrence onto pretrained models at test time. We show that naive block reapplication usually degrades performance, highlighting the importance of the loop application strategy. Motivated by viewing a pre-norm transformer block as a forward Euler step on an ODE, we instead treat looping as a refinement of the same approximation, replacing one large update with smaller damped sub-steps. Across seven dense, sparse MoE, and MLA+MoE model families, our method improves Qwen3-4B-Instruct by +2.64 pp on MMLU-Pro, Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct by +1.14 pp on CommonsenseQA, and Moonlight-16B-A3B-Instruct by +1.20 pp on OpenBookQA.

AI.tldr: Training-free looped transformers improve frozen model performance at inference time by wrapping a contiguous block of layers and applying damped sub-step updates, inspired by ODE Euler discretization, without any additional training or fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing looped transformer methods require end-to-end training with the looped structure, which is costly. The authors aim to retrofit recurrence onto frozen pretrained models at test time without fine-tuning or architectural changes.
AI.method: A lightweight inference-time wrapper loops a contiguous mid-stack block of layers from a frozen checkpoint. Motivated by viewing a pre-norm transformer block as a forward Euler step on an ODE, looping is treated as a refinement by replacing one large update with smaller damped sub-steps.
AI.result: Across seven dense, sparse MoE, and MLA+MoE model families: Qwen3-4B-Instruct improves +2.64 pp on MMLU-Pro, Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct +1.14 pp on CommonsenseQA, and Moonlight-16B-A3B-Instruct +1.20 pp on OpenBookQA.
AI.conclusion: Training-free looped transformers can effectively improve pretrained model performance at inference time without additional training, but the loop application strategy (damped sub-steps) is critical—naive block reapplication degrades performance.

title: Ultra-Granular Calorimeter Performances for the Heavy Flavor Physics Program at the Z Peak
summary: Various decays of the B mesons are here used to establish the performances of an ultra-granular electromagnetic calorimeter for heavy flavour physics at an electron positron accelerator running at the Z peak. The silicon-tungsten electromagnetic calorimeter of the ILD concept is used for this purpose, enhanced by a timing capability. When possible, a $π$ 0 mass fit of $γ$$γ$ system is performed to improve the $π$ 0 energy resolution. It is also shown that in the presence in the final state of a photon without a $π$ 0 , the identification of genuine photon(s) versus fake photon(s) coming from K L 's, neutron's, debris of hadronic shower or other high energy $π$ 0 , is essential. It allows for a good precision and a good signal over background ratio for this kind of physics. The possible impact for the tau physics is discussed.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates a high-granularity silicon-tungsten electromagnetic calorimeter with timing capability for B meson decays at a Z-peak electron-positron collider, focusing on π⁰ reconstruction via γγ mass fitting and photon identification to suppress fakes from KL, neutrons, hadronic showers, and high-energy π⁰s. The methods improve energy resolution and signal-to-background ratio, with potential benefits for tau physics.
AI.motivation: To assess and enhance the performance of an ultra-granular electromagnetic calorimeter for heavy flavour physics (B meson decays) at a Z-pole e⁺e⁻ collider, using the ILD concept's silicon-tungsten design with timing capability.
AI.method: Using various B meson decays as benchmarks; applying π⁰ mass fit to γγ systems to improve π⁰ energy resolution; developing identification methods to distinguish genuine photons from fakes (KL, neutrons, hadronic shower debris, high-energy π⁰s).
AI.result: The π⁰ mass fit improves π⁰ energy resolution; genuine photon identification significantly reduces backgrounds and improves signal-to-background ratio for B decay physics; the techniques show promise for tau physics as well.
AI.conclusion: An ultra-granular electromagnetic calorimeter with timing capability, combined with π⁰ mass fitting and photon-vs-fake discrimination, enables high-precision heavy flavour physics at a Z-pole collider, with potential applicability to tau physics.

title: Pressure and temperature relaxation limit for a one-velocity Baer-Nunziato model
summary: The dynamics of two-phase flows out of mechanical and thermal equilibrium are described by a partially dissipative first-order quasilinear system with stiff interaction terms associated with fast relaxation scales. In this paper, we analyze from a mathematical point of view the resulting pressure and temperature relaxation singular limit problem for a one velocity Baer-Nunziato model. This leads to a singular limit problem involving two small parameters. We propose a uniform symmetrization of this system which allows us to justify the strong relaxation limit and to establish a convergence rate for classical solutions.

AI.tldr: This paper mathematically analyzes the singular limit problem of pressure and temperature relaxation in a one-velocity Baer-Nunziato model for two-phase flows, providing uniform symmetrization to justify the strong relaxation limit and establish convergence rates.
AI.motivation: To mathematically understand the pressure and temperature relaxation singular limit problem in two-phase flow models with fast relaxation scales, which involves two small parameters and requires rigorous justification.
AI.method: The authors propose a uniform symmetrization of the partially dissipative quasilinear system describing two-phase flows, which enables them to analyze the singular limit problem involving two small parameters.
AI.result: The uniform symmetrization approach justifies the strong relaxation limit and establishes a convergence rate for classical solutions of the Baer-Nunziato model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mathematical framework successfully handles the complex multi-parameter singular limit problem in two-phase flow dynamics, providing rigorous convergence guarantees.

title: Norm inflation in negative order Sobolev spaces for KdV and KP
summary: We prove norm inflation phenomena for KdV and KP equations in negative order Sobolev spaces, in the periodic case, as well as on the whole space, on an arbitrarily large scale of negative order Sobolev spaces as target spaces. The proof relies on WKB analysis for a semiclassical version of the equation, in a weakly nonlinear r{é}gime, and the creation of the zero Fourier mode by resonant interaction. Unlike in previous similar results, this average mode has a smaller order of magnitude than the initial data, which requires a more detailed WKB analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proves norm inflation for KdV and KP equations in negative order Sobolev spaces by using WKB analysis and resonant interaction to create a zero Fourier mode, requiring more detailed analysis due to the mode's smaller magnitude compared to initial data.
AI.motivation: To study norm inflation phenomena for KdV and KP equations in negative order Sobolev spaces, extending previous results by handling cases where the zero Fourier mode has a smaller order of magnitude.
AI.method: WKB analysis for a semiclassical version of the equation in a weakly nonlinear regime, combined with creation of the zero Fourier mode via resonant interaction.
AI.result: Norm inflation is proven for KdV and KP equations in negative order Sobolev spaces, both periodic and on the whole space, across an arbitrarily large scale.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully proves norm inflation even when the average mode is smaller than initial data, extending the known results to more challenging regimes through refined WKB analysis.

title: Simple proofs for the existence of smooth solutions to a reaction-diffusion system modeling reversible chemistry
summary: We present in this work a very short proof for the existence, uniqueness and smoothness in dimensions $d\leq 3$ of the system of reaction diffusion $ \partial\_t a\_i - d\_i Δa\_i = (-1)^i (a\_1 a\_3 - a\_2 a\_4)$, where $a\_i \geq 0$ model the concentrations of chemical species undergoing a chemical reaction and diffusing (each with its diffusion rate $d\_i > 0$) in a bounded container.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a concise proof for the existence, uniqueness, and smoothness of solutions to a reaction-diffusion system (in dimensions d≤3) modeling chemical concentrations with specific reaction kinetics.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical foundations for a specific reaction-diffusion system (a1a3 - a2a4 kinetics) in bounded domains, proving well-posedness in low dimensions.
AI.method: A very short proof (likely using fixed-point theorems, energy estimates, or semigroup theory) to show existence, uniqueness, and smoothness of solutions for dimensions d≤3.
AI.result: Proves existence, uniqueness, and smoothness of solutions to the reaction-diffusion system in dimensions d≤3.
AI.conclusion: The reaction-diffusion system is well-posed (solutions exist uniquely and smoothly) in low-dimensional bounded domains, providing a strong theoretical basis for this chemical model.

title: Global estimates on the Brenier map
summary: Caffarelli's contraction theorem and the analogous Laplacian result in [arXiv:2411.12109, arXiv:2501.11382] are two examples of how log-Hessian bounds on probability densities yield estimates on the derivative of the corresponding Brenier map with optimal dimensional dependence. The main goal of this paper is to extend such phenomenon to a broader class of convex estimates such as norms.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Caffarelli-type contraction estimates from log-Hessian bounds to a broader class of convex estimates such as norms.
AI.motivation: To generalize the known phenomenon where log-Hessian bounds on probability densities yield optimal dimensional estimates on the derivative of the Brenier map, extending it to a wider class of convex estimates.
AI.method: The paper extends Caffarelli's contraction theorem and related Laplacian results to broader convex estimates beyond the standard settings.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that log-Hessian bounds on probability densities can yield estimates on the derivative of the corresponding Brenier map for a broader class of convex estimates (e.g., norms).
AI.conclusion: The phenomenon of obtaining optimal dimensional dependence from log-Hessian bounds can be extended beyond the classical settings to more general convex estimates.

title: Asymptotic behavior of solutions for the nonlinear Hartree equation involving the fractional Laplacian
summary: In this paper, we investigate the nonlocal problem \begin{equation*}\left\lbrace \begin{aligned} &A_{s} u=(|x|^{-(n-2s)}\ast u^{2_{s}^{\sharp}-1-ε})u^{2_{s}^{\sharp}-2-ε} \quad\quad\hspace{3.5mm} \mbox{in}\hspace{2mm}Ω,\\ &u>0\quad\quad \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\hspace{2mm}\mbox{in}\hspace{2mm}Ω,\\ &u=0\quad \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\hspace{2mm}\mbox{on}\hspace{2mm}\mathbb{R}^n\setminusΩ, \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation*} where $Ω$ is a smooth bounded domain in $\mathbb{R}^n$, $0<s<1$, $n\in(2s,\min\{6s,n+2s\})$, $ε>0$ small, $2_{s}^{\sharp}-1=(n+2s)/(n-2s)$ and $A_{s}$ stands for the fractional Laplace operator $(-Δ)^{s}$ in $Ω$ with outside zero Dirichlet boundary condition. The above problem is reduced to the subcritical fractional system $$ A_{s}u=u^{2_{s}^{\sharp}-2-ε}v,\hspace{2mm}A_{s}v=u^{2_{s}^{\sharp}-1-ε},\hspace{2mm}u,v>0\hspace{2mm}\mbox{in}\hspace{2mm}Ω\hspace{2mm}\mbox{and}\hspace{2mm}u=(-Δ)^sv=0\hspace{2mm}\mbox{on}\hspace{2mm}\mathbb{R}^n\setminusΩ.$$ For a general domain $Ω$ or domains with convexity, we first prove a uniform $L^1$ bound away from the boundary and a uniform $L^{\infty}$ bound near the boundary for positive solutions to the general fractional Hartree-type PDEs by applying the moving planes method and integral estimates for the convolution term.Among these results, we study the asymptotic behavior of solutions as $ε\rightarrow0$.These solutions are shown to blow-up at exactly one point $x_0$ and location of this point is characterized. In addition, the shape and exact rates for blowing-up are studied.Finally,we also establish the corresponding main results for solutions of the fractional Brezis-Nirenberg problem involving critical Hartree-type nonlinearity.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a nonlocal fractional Laplacian problem with critical Hartree-type nonlinearity in bounded domains, proving uniform bounds via moving planes method, and analyzing blow-up behavior as a parameter ε→0, including single-point blow-up, blow-up rates, and the location of the blow-up point. Results are also extended to the fractional Brezis-Nirenberg problem.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior and blow-up phenomena of solutions to fractional Hartree-type PDEs with critical nonlinearities, particularly as the subcritical parameter approaches the critical exponent.
AI.method: The problem is reduced to a subcritical fractional system. The moving planes method and integral estimates for the convolution term are used to prove uniform L¹ bounds away from the boundary and L^∞ bounds near the boundary. Asymptotic analysis is then employed to study blow-up.
AI.result: Positive solutions blow up at exactly one point as ε→0; the location of the blow-up point is characterized; precise blow-up rates and shape are obtained. Results also hold for the fractional Brezis-Nirenberg problem with critical Hartree nonlinearity.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete blow-up analysis for fractional Laplacian equations with Hartree-type critical nonlinearity, extending known results to the nonlocal setting.

title: Pointwise Estimates Near Singular Sets for Quasilinear Elliptic Equations

AI.tldr: This paper studies the removability of boundary singular sets for quasilinear elliptic equations on Finsler manifolds, using distance-type functions ρ₁ and ρ₂. The key estimate |u(x)| ≤ C ρ₁(x)^{-τ} near the singular set Γ=ρ₁⁻¹({0}) allows proving singularity removability and convergence of weak solutions as p⁺→1 to a limit solution of the 1-Laplacian equation.
AI.motivation: To understand and prove that boundary singular sets are removable for certain quasilinear elliptic equations on Finsler manifolds, specifically when the variable exponent p approaches 1.
AI.method: The authors derive a key estimate |u(x)| ≤ C ρ₁(x)^{-τ} near the singular set Γ, with constants C>0 and τ>0 converging to positive values as p⁺→1. Using this estimate, they prove singularity removability and then show convergence of weak solutions u_p (with p⁺→1) to a BV solution of the 1-Laplacian equation.
AI.result: The singularity at Γ is removable for weak solutions. Additionally, as p⁺→1, there exists a subsequence of weak solutions {u_{p_m}} that converges to a solution u ∈ BV ∩ L^{q+1} of the equation -Δ₁ u + |u|^{q-1}u = 0.
AI.conclusion: Boundary singular sets can be removed for the studied class of quasilinear elliptic equations on Finsler manifolds, and the limiting behavior as p→1 leads to a well-defined solution in the BV space.
